This file contains the full text content of all pages on delawarebankruptcy.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).


---

## The 341 Meeting of Creditors in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/341-meeting.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›341 Meeting

The 341 Meeting of Creditors in Delaware

What to expect at the 341 meeting of creditors in Delaware. Location, questions asked, what to bring, and how to prepare for your hearing in the District of Delaware.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is the 341 Meeting?

The 341 meeting of creditors is a required part of every bankruptcy case, named after 11 U.S.C. section 341. It is your one mandatory appearance in the bankruptcy process.

Location: 824 Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801

When: Approximately 30-40 days after your bankruptcy petition is filed

Duration: Typically 5-10 minutes for routine consumer cases

What to Bring

Valid government-issued photo ID (driver's license, passport, state ID)

Social Security card or other proof of SSN (W-2, SSA-1099, or IRS letter)

Copy of your bankruptcy petition and schedules

Most recent federal tax return

Recent pay stubs (last 60 days)

Bank statements (last 6 months) - bring copies in case the trustee requests them

Common Questions the Trustee Will Ask

Did you read and sign your bankruptcy petition and schedules?

Is everything in your petition true and correct?

Have you listed all of your assets?

Have you listed all of your debts?

Have you sold, transferred, or given away any property in the last 2 years?

Are you expecting any tax refunds?

Do you have any pending lawsuits, insurance claims, or inheritance?

Is there anything you would like to change or correct in your petition?

Key tip: Answer honestly and briefly. If you do not know the answer, say so. Your attorney will be present and can help guide you.

What Happens After

Chapter 7

Trustee has 60 days to file objections

If no objections, discharge enters ~60 days after meeting

Case closes shortly after discharge

Total: ~3-4 months from filing to discharge

Chapter 13

Plan confirmation hearing follows

Trustee reviews plan feasibility

Creditors may file objections to plan

Court confirms plan (typically months 2-4)

Plan payments continue for 3-5 years

341 Meeting Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the 341 meeting in Delaware?

The 341 meeting (named after 11 U.S.C. section 341) is a required hearing where the bankruptcy trustee and any creditors can ask you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and petition. In the District of Delaware, these meetings are held at 824 Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801.

What questions will the trustee ask?

Common questions include: Did you review your petition and schedules? Is everything accurate? Have you listed all your assets and debts? Have you transferred any property in the last 2 years? Do you have any pending lawsuits or inheritance claims? Are you expecting a tax refund?

What should I bring to the 341 meeting?

Bring: a valid government-issued photo ID, your Social Security card (or other proof of SSN), your most recent tax return, recent pay stubs, and copies of your bankruptcy petition and schedules. Your attorney will advise on any additional documents.

How long does the 341 meeting last?

Most 341 meetings last 5-10 minutes. The trustee asks a standard set of questions, and unless there are complications or creditor objections, the meeting is brief. Complex cases or cases with significant assets may take longer.

Do creditors actually attend the 341 meeting?

In most consumer cases, creditors do not attend. Occasionally, a creditor may appear to ask about specific property or debts, but this is uncommon. You should be prepared for questions from any creditor, but do not be surprised if none show up.

What happens after the 341 meeting?

After the 341 meeting, the trustee has 60 days to object to your discharge or file motions regarding your assets. If no objections are filed, your Chapter 7 discharge is typically entered about 60 days after the meeting. In Chapter 13, plan confirmation proceedings continue.

## The Automatic Stay in Delaware Bankruptcy
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/automatic-stay.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Automatic Stay

The Automatic Stay in Delaware Bankruptcy

Learn how the automatic stay protects Delaware residents when filing bankruptcy. What stops immediately, what does not, stay violations, and your rights under 11 U.S.C. section 362.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What the Automatic Stay Stops Immediately

Stopped by the Stay

Wage garnishment - Your employer must stop withholding garnished wages once notified of the filing

Foreclosure - All foreclosure proceedings and sales are halted, even if a sale date is already scheduled

Vehicle repossession - Creditors cannot repossess your car, truck, or other vehicles

Lawsuits - All pending civil lawsuits for debt collection are paused

Creditor calls and letters - All collection contacts must stop

Bank account levies - Creditors cannot freeze or seize your bank account

Utility shutoffs - Utilities cannot be disconnected for 20 days after filing

IRS collection - Tax liens and levies are paused (though the lien itself survives)

Eviction - In some circumstances, pending eviction actions are paused

NOT Stopped by the Stay

Child support collection - Domestic support obligations continue

Criminal proceedings - Criminal cases continue

Tax audits - The IRS can continue auditing (but cannot collect)

Pension loan repayment - Deductions continue

Paternity and custody - Family court proceedings continue

Domestic violence proceedings - Protective orders continue

Government regulatory actions - Police and regulatory powers continue

Post-judgment evictions - If the landlord already has a judgment for possession

Stay Violations - What to Do

If a creditor continues collection activity after you file bankruptcy in the District of Delaware, that is a violation of the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k). Common violations include continued phone calls, repossessing a vehicle after filing, proceeding with foreclosure, and continuing wage garnishment.

Steps to Take

Document everything. Save letters, record dates and times, take screenshots, keep voicemails.

Notify the creditor in writing. Inform them of your case number and filing date.

Notify your attorney. A motion for contempt can be filed with the court.

You may be entitled to damages. Actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

How Long Does the Automatic Stay Last?

Chapter 7

The stay lasts until your case is closed, dismissed, or discharge is granted - typically 3-4 months. After discharge, the discharge injunction permanently replaces the stay for discharged debts.

Chapter 13

The stay lasts for the duration of your 3-5 year repayment plan. This extended protection is one of Chapter 13's key advantages for saving homes and vehicles.

Repeat Filers: Reduced Stay Protection

If you had a case dismissed within the past year, the stay in your new case expires after 30 days unless you file a motion to extend it (section 362(c)(3)). Two or more prior dismissals means no automatic stay without a court order (section 362(c)(4)).

Delaware-Specific Considerations

Delaware is known as the corporate bankruptcy capital of the United States. While large corporate Chapter 11 cases dominate the district, consumer bankruptcies follow the same federal rules. Delaware also uses judicial foreclosure, giving homeowners more time to file.

Delaware uses judicial foreclosure, which typically takes approximately 6 months or longer. This affects how urgently you may need to file to stop a pending foreclosure sale.

Delaware's garnishment limit is 15% of disposable earnings - more protective than the federal standard of 25%. Filing bankruptcy stops garnishment immediately through the automatic stay.

Full Automatic Stay Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the automatic stay in bankruptcy?

The automatic stay is a federal court order under 11 U.S.C. section 362 that takes effect the instant you file bankruptcy in the District of Delaware. It immediately stops most collection actions including wage garnishment, foreclosure, repossession, lawsuits, creditor calls, and utility shutoffs.

What does the automatic stay stop?

The automatic stay stops: wage garnishment, foreclosure sales and proceedings, vehicle repossession, debt collection lawsuits, creditor phone calls and letters, bank account levies, utility disconnection (for 20 days), eviction (in some cases), and IRS collection activity.

What does the automatic stay NOT stop?

The automatic stay does not stop: child support and alimony collection (domestic support obligations), criminal proceedings, certain tax audits, pension plan loan repayments, and evictions where the landlord already had a judgment for possession before filing.

What happens if a creditor violates the automatic stay in Delaware?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k), you may be entitled to actual damages, attorney fees, and in some cases punitive damages. Document the violation, notify your attorney, and a motion for contempt can be filed with the bankruptcy court in the District of Delaware.

Does the automatic stay apply if I filed bankruptcy before?

If you had a case dismissed within the past year, the stay lasts only 30 days unless extended by court order (section 362(c)(3)). Two or more prior dismissals means no automatic stay at all without a court order (section 362(c)(4)).

How long does the automatic stay last?

In Chapter 7, the stay lasts until discharge (typically 3-4 months). In Chapter 13, it lasts for the duration of your 3-5 year repayment plan. The discharge injunction under section 524 permanently replaces the stay for discharged debts.

## Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/bank-account.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Bank Accounts

Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Delaware

Understanding how bankruptcy interacts with your bank accounts in Delaware can help you protect your money and avoid unexpected freezes or seizures.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bank Account Levies and the Automatic Stay

Before bankruptcy, judgment creditors can obtain a court order to levy (freeze and seize) your bank account in Delaware. The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay prohibits all new levies.

New levies: Completely blocked by the stay

Pending levies: May need a motion to release frozen funds

Government debts: Tax levies are paused but not permanently blocked by the stay

Bank Setoff Rights

If you owe money to the same bank where your accounts are held (credit card, loan, line of credit), the bank has a right of setoff - meaning they can take money from your deposit account to cover what you owe, even without a court order.

How to Protect Yourself

Before filing: Open a new account at a bank or credit union where you have no debts

Move direct deposit: Switch your payroll direct deposit to the new bank

Keep records: Do not move large sums that could look like a fraudulent transfer

Timing: Coordinate with your attorney to minimize risk around the filing date

Do not drain accounts or make large transfers without attorney guidance. Transfers of property before filing can be scrutinized as potential fraudulent conveyances.

Protecting Bank Deposits in Delaware

In Chapter 7, the trustee can claim non-exempt cash in your bank accounts on the date of filing. In Delaware:

Homestead exemption: $125,000 (does not protect cash)

Vehicle exemption: $15,000 (does not protect cash)

Federal exemptions: If you elect federal exemptions, the wildcard exemption can protect cash deposits

Many Delaware filers protect bank deposits by timing the filing when account balances are low (right after paying normal living expenses like rent, utilities, and food).

Delaware exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can creditors freeze my bank account in Delaware?

Yes. Before bankruptcy, a creditor with a judgment can levy (freeze and seize funds from) your bank account in Delaware. After you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately prohibits any new levies and may require release of frozen funds.

Should I open a new bank account before filing?

If you owe money to the same bank where you have checking or savings accounts, the bank may exercise its right of setoff - taking money from your account to cover the debt. Opening an account at a bank where you have no debts before filing is a common protective measure.

Will bankruptcy affect my bank account?

Filing bankruptcy does not close your bank accounts. However, a Chapter 7 trustee could claim non-exempt funds. In Delaware, check which exemptions protect your cash and bank deposits.

Prepare Before You File

Check your discharge eligibility and learn how to protect your assets.

Discharge Screener
Delaware Filing Checklist

## Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13 in Delaware?
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/buy-car-chapter-13.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Buy Car in Ch. 13

Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13 in Delaware?

Yes - but you need court permission first. Buying a car during Chapter 13 requires filing a motion, getting trustee approval, and showing the purchase fits your budget.

Quick answer: You can buy a car during Chapter 13 in Delaware, but you must file a Motion to Incur New Debt and get court/trustee approval before signing anything. The court will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary and affordable within your plan.

The Process: Step by Step

1

Get Pre-Approved

Find a lender willing to work with Chapter 13 debtors. Get the terms in writing - vehicle, price, interest rate, monthly payment.

2

File the Motion

File a Motion to Incur New Debt with the District of Delaware bankruptcy court. Include all purchase details and an updated budget.

3

Court Approval

The trustee reviews and may object. If no objection, the court typically grants the motion within 2-3 weeks. Do not sign anything until approved.

What the Court Wants to See

Necessity: Is the vehicle necessary for work, medical appointments, or family needs? Luxury vehicles are almost always denied.

Affordability: Can you make both the car payment AND your Chapter 13 plan payment? The court will review your current budget.

Reasonable terms: Interest rates above 20-25% may be questioned. The court wants to see you are not being exploited.

Vehicle value: A used car in the $5,000-$15,000 range is typical. Requests for new or expensive vehicles face heavier scrutiny.

Down payment source: The court may ask where the down payment is coming from. It should not come from plan payments or exempt funds earmarked for creditors.

Do NOT sign a purchase agreement, make a deposit, or take possession of a vehicle before the court grants your motion. Unauthorized debt during Chapter 13 can result in case dismissal.

Interest Rates and Lender Options

Borrowers in active Chapter 13 cases face limited lender options and higher interest rates. Typical options in Delaware include:

Credit unions: Often the best rates for Chapter 13 borrowers. Some Delaware credit unions specifically work with bankruptcy filers.

Subprime auto lenders: Companies like Capital One Auto Finance, Westlake Financial, and DriveTime specialize in lending to borrowers in bankruptcy. Rates range from 12-24%.

Buy-here-pay-here dealers: Available but typically the worst terms. Interest rates can exceed 25%, and vehicle quality is often poor.

Section 506 cramdown: If your current vehicle loan is more than 910 days old, you may be able to reduce the balance to the vehicle's value through your Chapter 13 plan rather than buying a new car.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I buy a car while in Chapter 13 in Delaware?

Yes, but you need court approval. You must file a motion with the bankruptcy court requesting permission to incur new debt. The trustee and court will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary, whether you can afford it within your plan, and whether the terms are reasonable.

Do I need trustee approval to buy a car in Delaware Chapter 13?

Yes. In Delaware, as in all states, you must get trustee approval before taking on new debt during Chapter 13. The trustee will review the purchase price, interest rate, monthly payment, and whether it fits within your budget and plan obligations.

What interest rate can I expect when buying a car during Chapter 13?

Interest rates for borrowers in Chapter 13 are typically higher - often 15-25% from subprime lenders. Some credit unions and specialized lenders work with Chapter 13 debtors at better rates. The court may reject a purchase with an unreasonably high interest rate.

How do I file a motion to buy a car in Chapter 13?

You or your attorney file a Motion to Incur New Debt with the bankruptcy court. The motion should include the vehicle details, purchase price, interest rate, monthly payment, and an updated budget showing you can afford both the car payment and your plan payment.

Can I lease a car during Chapter 13 in Delaware?

Leasing during Chapter 13 is treated the same as buying - you need court approval. Some courts view leases less favorably because they do not build equity. The total lease cost will be compared against purchasing.

What happens if I buy a car without court approval?

Taking on new debt without court approval is a violation of your Chapter 13 plan. The trustee may file a motion to dismiss your case or convert it to Chapter 7. The unauthorized debt could also be treated as fraudulent.

Related Topics

## Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/buying-a-house.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Buying a House

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Delaware

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent homeownership. Thousands of Americans buy homes after bankruptcy every year. Here are the waiting periods, requirements, and strategies for Delaware residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA Loan2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years from discharge

VA Loan2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with lender approval)

USDA Loan3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with approval)

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

The waiting period starts from the discharge date, not the filing date. For Chapter 7, that is typically 3-4 months after filing. For Chapter 13, it is at the end of the 3-5 year plan.

Steps to Prepare for Homeownership

1

Rebuild Credit

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge. Make small purchases and pay in full monthly. Consider a credit-builder loan. Target a 620+ score for FHA, 640+ for conventional.

2

Save for Down Payment

FHA requires 3.5% down with a 580+ score (10% with 500-579). Conventional requires 3-5%. VA and USDA offer zero-down options.

3

Stabilize Employment

Lenders want to see 2 years of stable employment. Consistent income documentation is critical. Self-employed borrowers may need additional tax returns.

Delaware Homestead Exemption

When you do buy a home in Delaware, the state homestead exemption protects $125,000 in home equity from future creditors. This means:

Your home equity up to $125,000 is protected from judgment creditors

If you ever need to file bankruptcy again, this equity is exempt from the Chapter 7 trustee

Delaware allows choosing between state and federal exemptions, giving flexibility in protecting your home

Learn more about keeping your house in Delaware bankruptcy →

Frequently Asked Questions

How long after bankruptcy can I buy a house in Delaware?

After Chapter 7: 2-4 years depending on the loan type (FHA requires 2 years, conventional requires 4 years). After Chapter 13: 1-2 years after discharge (FHA may allow applications during an active plan with trustee approval). VA loans require 2 years after Chapter 7 and may allow applications during Chapter 13.

Can I get a mortgage during Chapter 13?

Some lenders and the FHA allow mortgage applications during an active Chapter 13 plan if you have made 12 consecutive on-time payments and receive court and trustee approval. This is difficult but not impossible.

Will bankruptcy destroy my credit score?

Bankruptcy causes an initial credit score drop of 100-200 points. However, many Delaware filers see improvement within 12-18 months because the debt-to-income ratio improves and negative accounts stop accumulating late payments. Active rebuilding efforts accelerate recovery.

Start Planning Your Path to Homeownership

Check your discharge eligibility and learn about rebuilding credit after bankruptcy.

Discharge Screener
Rebuild Credit Guide

## Car Leases and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/car-lease.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Car Leases

Car Leases and Bankruptcy in Delaware

You have two options: assume the lease (keep the car) or reject it (return the car). The 60-day clock starts when you file.

Quick answer: When you file bankruptcy in Delaware, you must decide within 60 days whether to keep or surrender your leased vehicle. If you keep it, you must stay current. If you surrender it, remaining lease obligations become dischargeable unsecured debt.

Assume vs. Reject: Your Two Options

Assume (Keep the Car)

Continue making lease payments as normal

Cure any past-due payments

Lease continues as if bankruptcy never happened

Must not be a financial burden on your budget

Must decide within 60 days of filing

Good option if payments are affordable and you need the car

Reject (Return the Car)

Surrender the vehicle to the leasing company

Early termination fees become unsecured debt

Remaining lease payments become unsecured debt

Deficiency balance discharged in Chapter 7

Paid at reduced rate in Chapter 13 plan

Good option if payments are too high or car is not needed

The 60-Day Deadline

Under 11 U.S.C. section 365(d)(2), you have 60 days from the date of filing to assume or reject your car lease in Chapter 7. This deadline can be extended by the court for cause, but extensions are not automatic.

If you fail to act within 60 days, the lease is deemed rejected. This means you lose the vehicle and the remaining obligations become unsecured debt.

Mark your calendar: 60 days from your bankruptcy filing date is your deadline for the lease decision. Missing it means automatic rejection.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 Lease Treatment

Chapter 7

60-day assume/reject deadline applies

Rejected lease: deficiency balance discharged

Assumed lease: continues normally

No option to modify lease terms

Best if you want a clean break from a bad lease

Chapter 13

Decision made as part of your plan

Can cure past-due payments through plan

Rejected lease: deficiency paid through plan at reduced rate

More flexibility on timing

Best if you are behind on payments but want to keep the car

Practical Considerations

Mileage and wear: If you are over your mileage limit or the car has excess wear, rejecting the lease in bankruptcy eliminates those penalties. They become dischargeable unsecured debt.

Gap insurance: If the car is worth less than the payoff, gap insurance may cover the difference. Bankruptcy rejection does not trigger gap insurance - it covers total loss/theft only.

New lease after bankruptcy: You can lease a new car after bankruptcy, but expect higher deposits and potentially higher rates. Some Delaware dealers work with recent bankruptcy filers.

Automatic stay protection: While your case is pending, the leasing company cannot repossess the vehicle. If you plan to reject, you have time to arrange alternative transportation.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens to my car lease if I file bankruptcy in Delaware?

In both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13, you must decide whether to assume (keep) or reject (surrender) your car lease within 60 days of filing. If you assume, you keep the car and continue making payments. If you reject, you return the vehicle and any remaining balance is treated as unsecured debt.

What does it mean to assume a lease in bankruptcy?

Assuming a lease means you agree to keep the lease, stay current on all payments, and cure any past-due amounts. The lease continues as if bankruptcy was never filed. You must be current on payments and the vehicle must not be a financial burden.

What does it mean to reject a lease in bankruptcy?

Rejecting a lease means surrendering the vehicle to the lessor. Any early termination fees, remaining payments, or deficiency balance become unsecured debt that can be discharged in Chapter 7 or paid through a Chapter 13 plan at pennies on the dollar.

What is the 60-day deadline for lease decisions?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 365(d)(2), you must assume or reject an unexpired lease within 60 days of filing (for Chapter 7). If you do not act within 60 days, the lease is deemed rejected. In Chapter 13, you can assume or reject as part of your plan.

Can I get out of a bad car lease through bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes. Rejecting a car lease in bankruptcy is one of the cleanest ways to exit a bad lease. Early termination fees and remaining balance become unsecured debt. In Chapter 7, this debt is typically discharged entirely.

Will the leasing company repossess my car during bankruptcy?

No. The automatic stay prevents the leasing company from repossessing the vehicle once you file. However, you must make your decision within 60 days and stay current on payments if you want to keep the car.

Related Topics

Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/chapter-13-eligibility.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 13 Eligibility

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Delaware

Chapter 13 lets Delaware residents keep their property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Here are the eligibility requirements.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Core Eligibility Requirements

1. Regular Income

You must have regular income to fund a Chapter 13 repayment plan. Acceptable sources include:

Wages or salary

Self-employment income

Social Security benefits

Pension or retirement income

Rental income

Regular contributions from a spouse or household member

2. Debt Limits

Your total debts (secured plus unsecured) must not exceed $2,750,000. This combined limit was established by the Bankruptcy Threshold Adjustment and Technical Corrections Act of 2022.

3. Tax Filing

You must have filed all required federal and state tax returns for the four years before filing.

4. Credit Counseling

You must complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing.

Plan Length: 3 Years vs. 5 Years

Below Median Income

If your household income is at or below the Delaware median, your plan can be as short as 36 months (3 years) and no longer than 60 months (5 years).

Above Median Income

If your household income exceeds the Delaware median, your plan must be 60 months (5 years). This is called the applicable commitment period.

Prior Filing Bars for Chapter 13

Under 11 U.S.C. section 1328(f), you cannot receive a Chapter 13 discharge if:

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 4 years

You received a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 2 years

These time bars only prevent a discharge - you can still file Chapter 13 to use the automatic stay and structured repayment even without eligibility for a discharge.

Check your eligibility with the 1328(f) Screener →

Chapter 13 Advantages in Delaware

Keep your home: Catch up on mortgage arrears while protecting up to $125,000 in equity

Keep your car: Restructure auto loans (potential cramdown if older than 910 days) with $15,000 exemption protection

No means test barrier: Unlike Chapter 7, high earners can file Chapter 13

Co-debtor stay: Protects co-signers on consumer debts during the plan

Broader discharge: Some debts not dischargeable in Chapter 7 may be discharged in Chapter 13

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the debt limits for Chapter 13 in Delaware?

As of 2024, the debt limit for Chapter 13 is $2,750,000 in combined secured and unsecured debts. This limit was updated by the Bankruptcy Threshold Adjustment and Technical Corrections Act. If your debts exceed this amount, you may need to file Chapter 11 instead.

Do I need regular income to file Chapter 13?

Yes. Chapter 13 requires regular income sufficient to fund a repayment plan. This can include wages, salary, commissions, self-employment income, Social Security, pension, rental income, or even consistent support from a spouse or domestic partner.

How long is a Chapter 13 plan in Delaware?

If your income is below the Delaware median for your household size, your plan can be 3-5 years. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The plan cannot exceed 5 years (60 months).

Check Your Eligibility

See if prior filings affect your Chapter 13 discharge eligibility.

## Chapter 13 Payments in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/chapter-13-payments.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 13 Payments

Chapter 13 Payments in Delaware

Your Chapter 13 plan payment is based on your income, expenses, and debts. Here is how it works in Delaware and what to do if you cannot keep up.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Payments Are Calculated

Three tests determine your minimum Chapter 13 payment:

1. Disposable Income Test

Your monthly income minus reasonable living expenses (IRS standards and actual secured debt payments). All disposable income must go into the plan.

2. Liquidation Test (Best Interest of Creditors)

Unsecured creditors must receive at least what they would get if you filed Chapter 7. If you have significant non-exempt assets, this raises your payment.

3. Secured and Priority Debt Test

Your plan must pay all priority debts in full (taxes, child support arrears) and cure any secured debt arrears (mortgage, car loan). These are non-negotiable minimums.

Plan Length in Delaware

Below Median Income

36-60 months. Your plan can be as short as 3 years but cannot exceed 5 years. Shorter plans mean higher monthly payments but less time in bankruptcy.

Above Median Income

60 months (required). If your income exceeds the Delaware median for your household size, you must commit to a full 5-year plan.

What Gets Paid in a Chapter 13 Plan

Debt TypeTreatment

Mortgage arrearsPaid in full to cure default and keep home (homestead exemption: $125,000)

Car loanPaid through plan, possible cramdown if older than 910 days

Priority taxesPaid in full over plan length

Child support arrearsPaid in full

Unsecured debtPaid 0-100% depending on disposable income

Trustee feesTypically 5-10% of plan payments, built into payment amount

Missing Payments and Modification

If you fall behind on Chapter 13 payments:

Contact the trustee immediately - many will work with you on temporary hardship

File a plan modification if the change is permanent (job loss, pay cut, medical issues)

Hardship discharge may be available under section 1328(b) if you have paid the equivalent of what Chapter 7 creditors would have received

Convert to Chapter 7 if you no longer have sufficient income for any plan

Do not simply stop paying. Communicate with the trustee and your attorney to explore all options before your case is dismissed.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much will my Chapter 13 payment be in Delaware?

Your monthly payment depends on your disposable income (income minus allowed expenses), the amount of secured and priority debts you must pay, and the liquidation test (unsecured creditors must receive at least what they would get in Chapter 7). Most Delaware Chapter 13 payments range from a few hundred to a few thousand dollars per month.

What happens if I miss a Chapter 13 payment?

Missing payments can result in the trustee filing a motion to dismiss your case. If your case is dismissed, you lose the protection of the automatic stay and all the progress you have made. Contact the trustee immediately if you are going to be late - many allow temporary payment modifications.

Can I change my Chapter 13 payment amount?

Yes. You can file a modified plan with the D. Del. court if your financial situation changes. Common reasons include job loss, pay cut, medical emergency, or increased expenses. The modified plan must still meet all legal requirements.

Explore Your Options

Delaware Chapter 13 Guide

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Delaware - Payment Plans, Timeline
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/chapter-13.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Delaware

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Understanding the process and your Delaware exemptions is critical to success.

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

$125,000

Homestead Exemption

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13, sometimes called a "wage earner's plan," allows people with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3 to 5 years. You make monthly payments to a bankruptcy trustee, who distributes the money to your creditors according to the plan.

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not require liquidating assets. You keep all your property. At the end of the plan, remaining eligible debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments

Keeping a car while catching up on auto loan payments

Paying tax debts or other priority debts over time

Protecting non-exempt property that would be lost in Chapter 7

Filers who do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

How a Chapter 13 Plan Works

The Monthly Payment

Your monthly payment is calculated based on your disposable income - the difference between your income and reasonable living expenses. The payment must cover:

Priority debts in full: Recent taxes, child support arrears, attorney fees

Secured debt arrears: Mortgage arrears, car loan arrears

Trustee fees: Typically around 10% of plan payments

Unsecured creditors: Whatever remains goes to credit cards, medical bills - often pennies on the dollar

Plan Duration: 3 or 5 Years?

If your income is below the Delaware median, you may be able to complete a 3-year plan. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The court will not approve a plan longer than 5 years.

What Happens at the End?

After completing all plan payments, the court enters a discharge under 11 U.S.C. section 1328. This eliminates remaining eligible unsecured debts. You keep your home, car, and other property.

If you received a prior discharge, time bars under section 1328(f) may prevent you from receiving another discharge.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Complete credit counseling ($15-25). Gather income documentation, tax returns, and debt records.

Day 1 - Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments stop immediately.

Day 14: Proposed plan filed (if not filed with the petition).

Day 30: First plan payment due to the trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors. The trustee reviews your plan and finances.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing. The court decides whether to approve your plan.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to the trustee. Report income changes. File tax returns on time.

Plan completion: Complete debtor education course. Court enters discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long is a Chapter 13 repayment plan?

Chapter 13 plans last 3 to 5 years. If your income is below the Delaware median, you may qualify for a 3-year plan. If above the median, your plan must be 5 years. During this time, you make monthly payments to a trustee who distributes the money to your creditors.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. One of the main benefits of Chapter 13 is the ability to keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your repayment plan. The automatic stay stops foreclosure proceedings immediately upon filing. Delaware's homestead exemption of $125,000 also applies.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

If your Chapter 13 case is dismissed, the automatic stay ends and creditors can resume collection activity. You lose the protection of the bankruptcy court. Any payments you made through the plan were distributed to creditors, but you do not receive a discharge of your remaining debts. Learn more.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/chapter-7-eligibility.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 7 Eligibility

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Delaware

Chapter 7 bankruptcy can eliminate most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Here is how to determine whether you qualify to file in Delaware.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Means Test

The primary eligibility requirement for Chapter 7 is the means test under 11 U.S.C. section 707(b). It has two parts:

Step 1: Income Comparison

Compare your current monthly income (averaged over the 6 months before filing) to the Delaware median income for your household size. If your income is at or below the median, you automatically pass.

Step 2: Expense Deductions (If Above Median)

If your income exceeds the median, you deduct allowed expenses (IRS standards plus actual secured debt payments) from your income. If the remaining disposable income is below the threshold, you still qualify for Chapter 7.

Tip: Use the means test calculator to check your eligibility before paying for a consultation.

Prior Filing Bars

Under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a)(8) and (a)(9), you cannot receive a Chapter 7 discharge if:

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 8 years

You received a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 6 years (unless you paid 100% of unsecured claims or at least 70% in a good-faith plan)

Use the 1328(f) Screener to check whether prior filings affect your eligibility.

Other Eligibility Requirements

Credit counseling: Must complete an approved course within 180 days before filing

No recent dismissals for cause: If a case was dismissed in the past 180 days for violating a court order or failure to appear, you cannot file immediately

Domicile requirement: You file in the D. Del. if you have lived in Delaware for the greater portion of the past 180 days

No fraud: Fraudulent transfers or concealment of assets can bar discharge under section 727(a)(2)

Tax returns: You must provide your most recent tax return to the trustee at least 7 days before the 341 meeting

Delaware Exemption Snapshot

If you qualify for Chapter 7, your exempt property is protected from liquidation:

Homestead: $125,000

Vehicle: $15,000

Federal exemptions: Delaware permits choosing federal exemptions as an alternative

Complete Delaware exemptions list →

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the income limits for Chapter 7 in Delaware?

To qualify for Chapter 7 in Delaware, your household income must be at or below the state median income for your household size. If above median, you must pass the means test, which deducts allowed expenses from your income. If the result shows insufficient disposable income, you still qualify. Current Delaware median income figures are updated twice yearly.

What disqualifies you from filing Chapter 7?

You may be disqualified from Chapter 7 if: you received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 8 years, a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 6 years, your income is too high to pass the means test, you committed bankruptcy fraud, or you had a case dismissed within 180 days for specific reasons.

Do I have to take a class before filing Chapter 7 in Delaware?

Yes. You must complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. After filing, you must also complete a financial management course before receiving your discharge. Both courses can be done online and typically cost $25-$50 each.

Check Your Eligibility Now

Our free screener checks prior filings and time bars instantly.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Delaware - Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/chapter-7.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Delaware

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what Delaware residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors - but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

Delaware allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test in Delaware

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the Delaware median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility

For a single filer in Delaware, the current median is approximately $55,000/year. These figures increase with household size.

Delaware Exemptions in Chapter 7

Exemptions determine what property you keep.

PropertyDelaware Exemption

Homestead$125,000

VehicleNo specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal alternative: $4,450)

Household goodsNo specific limit under state law

Wildcard$500 (state); federal wildcard: $1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Tools of trade$15,000 (tools of trade)

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Full exemption details →

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation - Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing - Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately - creditors must stop all collection activity.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting - Brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath about your finances. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor education - Complete the second required financial education course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge - The court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (in most cases), recent taxes, child support, alimony, or debts incurred through fraud. See section 523(a) for the full list.

What is the means test for Chapter 7 in Delaware?

The means test compares your household income to the Delaware median. If your income is below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify. If above, a more detailed calculation determines whether you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan instead. For a single filer in Delaware, the current median is approximately $55,000/year.

How long does Chapter 7 take?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting of creditors occurs about 30 days after filing, and the discharge is usually entered about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7 in Delaware?

Delaware exemptions protect: homestead equity up to $125,000, vehicle equity up to No specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal alternative: $4,450), household goods up to No specific limit under state law, and retirement accounts are fully exempt. Full exemption details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy Filing Checklist for Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/checklist.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Checklist

Bankruptcy Filing Checklist for Delaware

Complete checklist for filing bankruptcy in Delaware. Documents needed, steps to take, and what to expect before, during, and after filing in the District of Delaware.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Before Filing

Complete credit counseling course from a U.S. Trustee-approved agency (within 180 days of filing)

Gather tax returns for the last 2 years

Collect pay stubs for the last 6 months

Obtain bank statements for the last 6 months (all accounts)

List all debts with creditor names, account numbers, and current balances

List all assets with current values (home, vehicles, personal property, investments)

Obtain a current credit report from annualcreditreport.com

Gather vehicle titles and registration documents

Collect mortgage and home equity statements

Gather proof of all income sources

Document monthly expenses (rent/mortgage, utilities, food, transportation, insurance)

List any property transfers made in the last 2 years

Note any lawsuits, garnishments, or collection actions pending against you

Things to Avoid Before Filing

Do not transfer property - Transferring assets to friends or family within 2 years of filing can be reversed by the trustee as a fraudulent transfer

Do not pay back family preferentially - Payments to family members within 1 year can be recovered by the trustee as preferential transfers

Do not take on new debt - Incurring debt you know you cannot repay may be considered fraud

Do not drain retirement accounts - Retirement funds are generally protected in bankruptcy; do not spend them on debts

Do not hide assets - Failure to disclose assets is a federal crime and will result in denial of discharge

Do not make large credit card purchases - Charges over $800 for luxury goods within 90 days of filing are presumed non-dischargeable

Filing Day

Petition and schedules filed with the District of Delaware

Filing fee paid ($338 Chapter 7 / $313 Chapter 13) or installment plan requested

Credit counseling certificate filed with petition

Automatic stay takes effect immediately

Case number assigned

Trustee assigned

341 meeting date scheduled (approximately 30-40 days out)

After Filing

Notify your attorney of any creditor contact (automatic stay violations)

Prepare for 341 meeting of creditors

Bring photo ID and Social Security proof to 341 meeting

Complete debtor education course (required before discharge)

File certificate of debtor education with the court

Continue making any required payments (mortgage, car, Chapter 13 plan)

Respond to any trustee requests for additional documents

Await discharge order (Chapter 7: ~90-120 days; Chapter 13: after plan completion)

How to File Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

What documents do I need to file bankruptcy in Delaware?

You need: tax returns (last 2 years), pay stubs (last 6 months), bank statements (last 6 months), a list of all debts with account numbers and balances, vehicle titles and registration, mortgage statement, proof of insurance, and a credit report. You also need a certificate of completion from an approved credit counseling agency.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 and Chapter 13 filing fee is $313. Attorney fees vary. Both chapters require two credit counseling courses ($25-$50 total). Filing fee waivers or installment plans may be available for those who qualify.

What should I NOT do before filing bankruptcy?

Do not: transfer property to friends or family, pay back family members preferentially, take on new debt, hide assets, drain retirement accounts to pay debts, or make large purchases on credit. These actions can jeopardize your case or result in denial of discharge.

Do I need a lawyer to file bankruptcy?

While you can file pro se (without a lawyer), bankruptcy is complex and mistakes can be costly. An attorney ensures your exemptions are properly claimed, your petition is accurate, and your rights are protected throughout the process in the District of Delaware.

What is credit counseling?

Federal law requires two courses: (1) credit counseling before filing (must be completed within 180 days before filing), and (2) debtor education after filing (must be completed before discharge). Both must be from agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee's office.

How do I find my local bankruptcy court in Delaware?

The bankruptcy court for the District of Delaware is located at 824 Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801. Filing is typically done electronically by your attorney. Pro se filers can file in person at the clerk's office.

Alaska
Arizona
Arkansas
California

Florida

## Converting Bankruptcy Chapters in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/converting-chapters.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Converting Chapters

Converting Bankruptcy Chapters in Delaware

If your financial situation changes after filing, Delaware debtors can convert between bankruptcy chapters. Understanding your conversion rights can help you get the best outcome.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Conversion Options

Chapter 7 to Chapter 13

One-time right of conversion under section 706(a)

Common when you have assets at risk of liquidation

Useful when income increases or you want to save property

Must be able to propose a feasible repayment plan

No additional filing fee

Chapter 13 to Chapter 7

Right to convert at any time under section 1307(a)

Not available if case was originally Chapter 7 and converted to 13

Common when income drops and plan payments become unaffordable

Must still pass the means test if required

No additional filing fee

Common Reasons for Conversion in Delaware

Job loss or income reduction - Chapter 13 plan payments become unaffordable, convert to Chapter 7

Income increase - No longer pass the means test for Chapter 7, convert to Chapter 13

Property at risk - Chapter 7 trustee identifies non-exempt property, convert to Chapter 13 to protect it

Mortgage arrears - Need Chapter 13's structured repayment to catch up on missed mortgage payments and keep your home (homestead exemption: $125,000)

Plan failure - Unable to complete Chapter 13 plan, convert to Chapter 7 for immediate discharge

What Happens When You Convert

File a motion or notice of conversion with the D. Del. bankruptcy court

New schedules may be required - particularly if significant time has passed or circumstances have changed

New trustee is assigned - Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 trustees are different

The automatic stay continues - no gap in protection

A new 341 meeting may be scheduled - the new trustee needs to examine you

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I convert my bankruptcy case to a different chapter?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 706 and section 1307, you generally have the right to convert between chapters. Chapter 7 debtors can convert to Chapter 13 once as a matter of right. Chapter 13 debtors can convert to Chapter 7 at any time if they have not previously converted from Chapter 7.

Does converting restart my case?

Converting does not start a completely new case. Your case number stays the same, and the automatic stay remains in effect. However, new schedules and forms may need to be filed, and certain deadlines reset.

Are there fees to convert?

There is no additional filing fee to convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7. Converting from Chapter 7 to Chapter 13 requires paying the difference in filing fees ($313 minus $338, so no additional fee). Attorney fees may apply for the additional work.

Not Sure Which Chapter Is Right?

Compare the two main consumer bankruptcy chapters side by side.

Ch. 7 vs 13 Guide
Discharge Screener

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Delaware? - Filing Fees, Attorney Fees
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/cost.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Delaware?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown of every cost you should expect.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$1,200 to $2,000Typical Delaware range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Credit report$0 - $30Free from annualcreditreport.com

Fee Waiver (Chapter 7 Only)

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely. You file the fee waiver application (Official Form 103B) with your petition.

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$3,000 to $4,500Typical Delaware range; mostly paid through plan

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

The Chapter 13 Payment Advantage

Attorney fees and the filing fee can be included in your repayment plan. You typically need only a small upfront payment (often $0-$500), with the rest paid through your monthly plan payments over 3-5 years.

Ways to Reduce Costs

Free and Low-Cost Options

Delaware Volunteer Legal Services - Pro bono legal assistance for low-income Delaware residents.

Legal Services Corporation of Delaware - Free civil legal help for qualifying residents.

Fee waiver - eliminates the $338 Chapter 7 filing fee for income below 150% of poverty

Installment payments - pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days

Pro se filing - filing without an attorney is possible but not recommended for most filers

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Delaware?

The Chapter 7 filing fee is $338. Attorney fees in Delaware typically range from $1,200 to $2,000. Credit counseling courses cost about $25-50 total (two required). Fee waivers are available if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. National cost comparison.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Delaware?

The Chapter 13 filing fee is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $3,000 to $4,500 in Delaware. The filing fee and attorney fees can be paid through your repayment plan, so you usually do not need to pay everything upfront.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

The court filing fee can be waived in Chapter 7 if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Legal aid organizations in Delaware may provide free or reduced-cost legal representation for qualifying individuals.

Do I have to pay an attorney upfront?

For Chapter 7, most attorneys require full payment before filing. For Chapter 13, attorney fees are typically included in your repayment plan, so you may only need a small upfront payment.

## Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/credit-after-bankruptcy.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Credit After Bankruptcy

Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Delaware

How to rebuild your credit after filing bankruptcy in Delaware. Timeline, secured cards, auto loans, mortgages, and practical steps for a fresh start.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Credit Recovery Timeline

Month 1-3: Foundation

Get your free credit reports from annualcreditreport.com. Dispute any errors. All discharged debts should show $0 balance. Apply for a secured credit card.

Month 3-6: Building

Use your secured card for small recurring purchases (gas, subscriptions). Pay the full balance every month. Never use more than 30% of your limit. Consider a credit-builder loan from a credit union.

Month 6-12: Growing

Your score should start climbing. You may qualify for a second credit card. Continue 100% on-time payment history. Some auto lenders will consider you for a loan.

Year 1-2: Accelerating

Scores in the 600-650 range are common with disciplined credit use. Auto loan rates improve significantly. Some unsecured credit cards become available.

Year 2-4: Normalizing

FHA mortgage loans become available (2 years post-Chapter 7). Scores can reach 700+ with consistent effort. The bankruptcy's impact diminishes each year.

Practical Steps for Delaware Residents

Secured Credit Cards

The fastest path to rebuilding credit. You deposit $200-$500, which becomes your credit limit. Use it for one or two small purchases per month and pay the full balance. After 6-12 months of responsible use, many issuers will upgrade you to an unsecured card and refund your deposit.

Credit-Builder Loans

Available at many Delaware credit unions and community banks. You make fixed monthly payments into a savings account. Once the loan is paid off, you receive the funds. Every on-time payment is reported to all three credit bureaus.

Authorized User Strategy

If a trusted family member adds you as an authorized user on a credit card with a long positive history, that history can appear on your credit report. You do not need to use the card - the positive history alone helps your score.

Auto Loans After Bankruptcy

Many Delaware auto lenders work with post-bankruptcy buyers. Expect higher rates initially (10-18%), but these improve with time. A larger down payment (10-20%) helps secure better terms. Refinancing after 12 months of on-time payments can reduce your rate significantly.

Mortgage After Bankruptcy in Delaware

FHA Loans

2 years after Chapter 7 discharge

1 year into Chapter 13 plan (with court approval)

3.5% minimum down payment

Delaware median home prices apply

Conventional Loans

4 years after Chapter 7 discharge

2 years after Chapter 13 discharge

Higher credit score required (620+)

Better rates than FHA long-term

Credit Rebuilding Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit report in Delaware?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years from the filing date. However, the impact on your credit score diminishes significantly after 2-3 years of responsible credit use.

Can I get a credit card after bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes. Many people qualify for secured credit cards within weeks of receiving their discharge. A secured card requires a deposit (usually $200-$500) that becomes your credit limit. Use it for small purchases and pay the full balance monthly.

How soon can I buy a house after bankruptcy in Delaware?

FHA loans are available 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge or 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan (with court approval and good payment history). Conventional loans typically require a 4-year wait after Chapter 7. VA loans require 2 years.

How soon can I buy a car after bankruptcy in Delaware?

Many people can get an auto loan immediately after discharge, though interest rates will be higher initially. Rates improve significantly after 12-24 months of on-time payments on other accounts.

What credit score can I expect after bankruptcy?

Most people see their credit score drop to the 450-550 range immediately after filing. With consistent effort - secured cards, on-time payments, low utilization - scores in the 650-700 range are achievable within 18-24 months.

Should I use a credit repair company after bankruptcy?

Generally no. Credit repair companies cannot remove accurate bankruptcy information from your report. You can dispute errors yourself for free through annualcreditreport.com. Focus on building positive payment history instead.

## Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/credit-card-debt.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Credit Card Debt

Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Delaware

Credit card debt is one of the most common reasons Delaware residents file for bankruptcy. In most cases, credit card balances can be completely eliminated through Chapter 7 or significantly reduced through Chapter 13.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Each Chapter Handles Credit Card Debt

Chapter 7

Credit card debt is eliminated entirely in Chapter 7. The discharge typically comes in 3-4 months. You must pass the means test to qualify. Any non-exempt assets could be liquidated, but most Delaware filers keep all their property under the state's exemptions.

Chapter 13

Credit card debt is included in your 3-5 year repayment plan. You pay a percentage of the total based on your disposable income. Many filers pay only 10-30 cents on the dollar. The remaining balance is discharged at the end of the plan.

Recent Charges: The 90-Day and 70-Day Rules

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C), certain recent credit card activity is presumed to be non-dischargeable:

Luxury goods/services over $800 charged within 90 days before filing

Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days before filing

These are rebuttable presumptions. The credit card company must file an adversary proceeding to challenge discharge, and you can present evidence that the charges were made with the intent and ability to repay.

Best practice: Stop using credit cards well before filing. Using cards when you know you will file bankruptcy can be considered fraud.

Credit Card Lawsuits and Judgments in Delaware

If a credit card company sues you in Delaware and obtains a judgment, they can:

Garnish your wages

Levy your bank account

Place a lien on your property

Filing bankruptcy stops all of these through the automatic stay. Pre-existing judgment liens on personal property can often be avoided (removed) under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f) if they impair your Delaware exemptions.

Delaware Exemptions Relevant to Credit Card Debt

Homestead: $125,000 - protects home equity from credit card judgment liens

Vehicle: $15,000

Federal exemptions: Available in Delaware as an alternative to state exemptions

Full Delaware exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge credit card debt in Delaware bankruptcy?

Yes. Credit card debt is unsecured debt and is generally dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Delaware. In Chapter 7, it is eliminated entirely. In Chapter 13, you pay a percentage based on your disposable income over 3-5 years, and the rest is discharged.

Will recent credit card charges be discharged?

Charges of over $800 for luxury goods or services within 90 days of filing, and cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days of filing, are presumed non-dischargeable under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C). The creditor must object for this to apply.

Can creditors sue me for credit card debt?

Yes. In Delaware, credit card companies can sue you, obtain a judgment, and garnish your wages or levy your bank account. Filing bankruptcy stops these actions through the automatic stay.

Ready to Explore Your Options?

Find out if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge to eliminate your credit card debt.

Discharge Screener
Cost Calculator

## Using Credit Cards Before Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/credit-cards-before-filing.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Credit Cards Before Filing

Using Credit Cards Before Bankruptcy in Delaware

Stop using credit cards once you decide to file bankruptcy. Charges made near filing can be challenged as fraud - and the presumption works against you.

Quick answer: Do not use credit cards once you decide to file bankruptcy. Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days and cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are presumed fraudulent. These debts may survive your discharge.

The Two Presumption Periods

90-Day Rule: Luxury Goods

Applies to purchases of luxury goods/services

Threshold: $800+ to a single creditor

Within 90 days of filing

Presumed non-dischargeable (you must prove otherwise)

11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C)(i)(I)

70-Day Rule: Cash Advances

Applies to cash advances

Threshold: $1,100+ in aggregate

Within 70 days of filing

Presumed non-dischargeable (you must prove otherwise)

11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C)(i)(II)

What You Should Do

Stop Using Cards Immediately

The moment you decide to file bankruptcy, stop all credit card use. Here is why:

Every charge creates risk. Even necessary purchases can be challenged if made close to filing.

Intent matters. Using a credit card when you know you will not repay it is the definition of fraud in this context.

The burden shifts to you. Within the 90/70 day windows, YOU must prove the charges were not fraudulent. Outside those windows, the creditor must prove fraud.

What Charges Are Safe?

Groceries and essential food - not luxury, reasonably necessary

Gas for work commute - necessary for income

Medical expenses - necessary for health

Basic utilities - if auto-billed to the card, switch to bank payment

Even "safe" charges should be avoided if possible. Use cash or debit instead. The cleaner your pre-filing credit card activity, the smoother your case.

What Happens If Charges Are Challenged

If a credit card company believes you ran up charges with no intent to repay, they can file an adversary proceeding - a lawsuit within your bankruptcy case. The process:

Creditor files a complaint alleging non-dischargeability under section 523(a)(2)

You must respond within 30 days

Discovery phase: both sides exchange evidence (statements, purchase records)

Trial or settlement: most adversary proceedings settle before trial

If the creditor wins, those specific charges survive your discharge

Adversary proceedings are expensive and time-consuming for both sides. Creditors typically only file them for significant amounts ($2,000+). But avoiding the risk entirely is the smartest strategy.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I use credit cards before filing bankruptcy in Delaware?

Technically you can, but it is strongly discouraged. Charges made with no intent to repay can be challenged as fraud. Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days of filing are presumed fraudulent. Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are also presumed fraudulent.

What is the 90-day rule for credit cards before bankruptcy?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C), charges for luxury goods or services exceeding $800 made within 90 days of filing bankruptcy are presumed to be non-dischargeable. The creditor does not have to prove fraud - you must prove the charges were not fraudulent.

What is the 70-day rule for cash advances?

Cash advances exceeding $1,100 taken within 70 days of filing are presumed non-dischargeable under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C). This includes balance transfers, convenience checks, and ATM cash advances.

What counts as a luxury purchase?

Luxury goods and services are items not reasonably necessary for support or maintenance. Groceries, gas, medical expenses, and basic clothing are not luxury. Electronics, jewelry, vacations, expensive dining, and entertainment are typically considered luxury.

What happens if I used credit cards right before filing?

The credit card company may file an adversary proceeding arguing the charges are non-dischargeable due to fraud. If the charges are within the 90/70 day presumption periods, you bear the burden of proving they were not fraudulent. Charges outside those windows require the creditor to prove actual fraud.

Should I close my credit cards before filing bankruptcy?

Do not close accounts before filing - it does not help and can look like you are trying to hide something. Simply stop using the cards. They will be listed in your bankruptcy schedules and the issuers will close them after receiving notice of your filing.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment

## Types of Debt in Delaware Bankruptcy
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/debt-types.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Debt Types

Types of Debt in Delaware Bankruptcy

Which debts can be discharged in Delaware bankruptcy? Secured vs unsecured, priority vs non-priority, and non-dischargeable debts explained.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Debt Categories in Bankruptcy

Secured Debt

Backed by collateral. The creditor has a lien on specific property.

Mortgage (secured by your home)

Car loan (secured by vehicle)

Home equity loan / HELOC

Secured personal loans

In bankruptcy: The lien survives even if personal liability is discharged. You must continue paying to keep the property.

Unsecured Debt

No collateral. The creditor has no claim on specific property.

Credit cards

Medical bills

Personal loans

Utility bills

Collection accounts

In bankruptcy: Typically discharged in full (Chapter 7) or partially paid through your plan (Chapter 13).

Priority Debts

Priority debts receive special treatment in bankruptcy and must be paid before general unsecured creditors. In Chapter 13, priority debts must be paid in full through your repayment plan.

Domestic support obligations - Child support and alimony (highest priority)

Administrative expenses - Trustee fees, attorney fees for the case

Recent tax debts - Income taxes within 3 years, trust fund taxes

Employee wages - Up to $15,150 per employee earned within 180 days before filing

Non-Dischargeable Debts

Certain debts survive bankruptcy and cannot be eliminated regardless of which chapter you file. These are listed in 11 U.S.C. sections 523(a) and 1328(a):

Student loans - Unless you prove undue hardship in an adversary proceeding

Child support and alimony - Domestic support obligations are never dischargeable

Recent taxes - Tax debts that do not meet the 3-year/2-year/240-day rules

Fraud - Debts obtained through fraud, false pretenses, or false financial statements

DUI debts - Debts arising from death or personal injury caused by driving under the influence

Criminal fines and restitution - Court-ordered penalties from criminal cases

Willful and malicious injury - Debts from intentional harm to another person or property

Non-Dischargeable Debts Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts can be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most unsecured debts can be discharged including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, utility bills, and old lease obligations. Secured debts (mortgage, car loan) can be discharged but the lien survives. Some debts like student loans require proving undue hardship.

What debts cannot be discharged?

Non-dischargeable debts include: most student loans (without proving undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (within 3 years), debts from fraud or misrepresentation, DUI-related debts, criminal fines and restitution, and debts from willful and malicious injury.

What is the difference between secured and unsecured debt?

Secured debt is backed by collateral (mortgage secured by your home, car loan secured by your car). Unsecured debt has no collateral (credit cards, medical bills). In bankruptcy, secured creditors have rights to the collateral; unsecured creditors typically receive little or nothing.

What are priority debts in bankruptcy?

Priority debts must be paid in full before other unsecured debts receive anything. They include: domestic support obligations (child support, alimony), recent tax debts, wages owed to employees (up to a limit), and certain other claims specified in 11 U.S.C. section 507.

Can I discharge credit card debt in Delaware?

Yes. Credit card debt is general unsecured debt and is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. However, charges made within 90 days of filing that exceed $800 for luxury goods, or cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days, may be presumed non-dischargeable.

What happens to cosigned debts in bankruptcy?

In Chapter 7, your discharge eliminates your obligation but the cosigner remains fully liable. In Chapter 13, the codebtor stay (section 1301) temporarily protects cosigners from collection while your plan is active. If you pay the cosigned debt in full through your plan, the cosigner is protected permanently.

## Bankruptcy Disqualifications in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/disqualifications.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Disqualifications

Bankruptcy Disqualifications in Delaware

Not everyone qualifies for bankruptcy relief. Understanding the disqualifications can help Delaware residents plan their filing strategy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Bars: When You Cannot File

Prior Discharge Time Bars

Prior DischargeNew Chapter 7New Chapter 13

Chapter 78 years4 years

Chapter 136 years*2 years

*The 6-year bar for Ch.13-to-Ch.7 is waived if you paid 100% of unsecured claims or at least 70% in a good-faith plan.

Use the 1328(f) Screener to calculate your exact eligibility dates.

Grounds for Denial of Discharge

Under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a), the court can deny your Chapter 7 discharge entirely if:

Concealment or transfer of property with intent to defraud within 1 year before filing or during the case (section 727(a)(2))

Destruction or concealment of records relating to your financial condition (section 727(a)(3))

Perjury or false statements in connection with the case (section 727(a)(4))

Failure to explain loss of assets or deficiency of assets (section 727(a)(5))

Failure to complete the post-filing financial management course (section 727(a)(11))

Prior discharge within the applicable time period (section 727(a)(8) and (a)(9))

Credit Counseling Requirement

You cannot file bankruptcy in D. Del. without first completing a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. There is no exception except:

Exigent circumstances that merit a waiver (very rarely granted)

The approved providers for your district are unable to provide services (almost never happens)

Courses can be completed online, by phone, or in person and typically take about one hour. A second course (debtor education / financial management) is required after filing but before discharge.

180-Day Dismissal Bar

Under 11 U.S.C. section 109(g), you cannot file a new bankruptcy petition if a prior case was dismissed within the past 180 days for:

Willful failure to obey orders of the court

Willful failure to appear before the court

Filing a motion to dismiss after a creditor requested relief from the automatic stay

If you have had a recent dismissal, count the days carefully. Filing before the 180-day period expires can result in immediate dismissal of the new case.

Means Test Disqualification (Chapter 7 Only)

If your income is above the Delaware median and you do not pass the means test expense deductions, you cannot file Chapter 7. Your options are:

File Chapter 13 instead (no income ceiling)

Wait until your income drops (e.g., job change, retirement)

Recalculate after 6 months if a high-income period falls out of the lookback window

Frequently Asked Questions

What disqualifies you from filing bankruptcy in Delaware?

You may be disqualified from bankruptcy if: you did not complete credit counseling, you received a recent prior discharge (8 years for Ch.7-to-Ch.7, 4 years for Ch.7-to-Ch.13, 6 years for Ch.13-to-Ch.7, 2 years for Ch.13-to-Ch.13), you committed fraud, your prior case was dismissed within 180 days for cause, or your income is too high for Chapter 7 and debts exceed Chapter 13 limits.

Can I be denied a bankruptcy discharge?

Yes. Even if you are allowed to file, the court can deny discharge for cause under section 727(a). Grounds include concealing assets, destroying records, committing perjury, failing to explain loss of assets, or failing to complete the financial management course.

What happens if my bankruptcy was recently dismissed in Delaware?

If a case was dismissed within the past 180 days for willful failure to obey a court order, appear before the court, or properly prosecute the case, you cannot file a new case immediately. Additionally, repeat filings within one year can limit or eliminate automatic stay protection.

Check Your Eligibility Now

Our free screener checks prior filing bars and time limits instantly.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide
