## Divorce and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/divorce-and-bankruptcy.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Divorce & Bankruptcy

Divorce and Bankruptcy in Delaware

Divorce and bankruptcy often occur together. Understanding how they interact in Delaware can help you protect your assets, manage shared debts, and plan the best filing strategy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Order: Bankruptcy Before, During, or After Divorce

1

Before Divorce

Filing a joint bankruptcy before divorce can eliminate shared unsecured debt and simplify the divorce property settlement. You save on filing fees by filing one joint case instead of two individual cases.

2

During Divorce

You can file bankruptcy during divorce proceedings. The automatic stay will pause any debt-related orders but does not affect custody, support, or the divorce itself. Coordination between courts is needed.

3

After Divorce

Filing after divorce means you file individually. Your divorce decree does not override bankruptcy law - a creditor can still come after you for joint debts even if the decree assigns them to your ex-spouse.

What Bankruptcy Cannot Discharge in Divorce

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(5) and (a)(15), the following divorce-related debts are not dischargeable:

Child support - all arrears and ongoing obligations

Alimony / spousal support - all arrears and ongoing obligations

Property settlement obligations - debts to a former spouse under a divorce decree, separation agreement, or property settlement (non-dischargeable in Chapter 7; potentially dischargeable in Chapter 13)

Hold-harmless agreements - if you agreed to pay a joint debt in the divorce, that obligation survives Chapter 7

Key difference: In Chapter 13, property division debts (section 523(a)(15)) may be dischargeable, while domestic support obligations (section 523(a)(5)) are never dischargeable.

Joint Debts and the Co-Debtor Stay

When one spouse files Chapter 13, the co-debtor stay under 11 U.S.C. section 1301 temporarily protects the non-filing spouse from creditors trying to collect on joint consumer debts. This protection does not exist in Chapter 7.

This is particularly important in Delaware where both spouses may be on the mortgage, car loan, or credit cards. The co-debtor stay lasts as long as the Chapter 13 plan is active.

Delaware Property Considerations

Homestead exemption: $125,000 - the bankruptcy and divorce courts both consider home equity

Vehicle exemption: $15,000

Federal exemptions available: Delaware allows choosing federal exemptions, which may be better depending on your assets

See the full Delaware exemptions guide for details.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file bankruptcy during a divorce in Delaware?

Yes. You can file bankruptcy before, during, or after a divorce in Delaware. The timing affects how debts and property are divided. Filing before the divorce can simplify property division by eliminating joint unsecured debt.

Does bankruptcy discharge alimony or child support?

No. Domestic support obligations including alimony and child support are not dischargeable in bankruptcy under any chapter. They are priority debts that must be paid in full.

What happens to joint debts in bankruptcy?

If one spouse files bankruptcy and discharges a joint debt, the other spouse remains fully liable. The creditor can pursue the non-filing spouse for the entire balance. A Chapter 13 co-debtor stay can temporarily protect the non-filing spouse during the repayment plan.

Planning Your Next Step

If you are considering bankruptcy alongside divorce, check your discharge eligibility first.

Discharge Screener
Chapter 7 Guide

## Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/emergency-bankruptcy.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Emergency Filing

Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in Delaware

When foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment is days or even hours away, Delaware residents can file an emergency bankruptcy petition to trigger the automatic stay immediately.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is an Emergency Bankruptcy Filing?

An emergency bankruptcy filing - sometimes called a bare-bones or skeleton filing - is when you file only the minimum required documents to open a case and activate the automatic stay. You file the rest within 14 days.

Minimum Documents Required

Voluntary Petition - the two-page form that officially opens the case

Creditor Matrix - a list of all known creditors with names and addresses

Credit Counseling Certificate - proof of the required pre-filing credit counseling course (can be completed online in about an hour)

When to Consider Emergency Filing in Delaware

Emergency Filing Makes Sense When

A foreclosure sale is scheduled within days

Your employer received a garnishment order that starts next pay period

A creditor is actively repossessing your vehicle

A bank account levy is about to clear

A lawsuit judgment is about to be enforced

Utility shutoff is imminent

Better to File a Complete Petition When

You have several weeks before any deadline

You want to avoid the risk of missing the 14-day supplement deadline

Your financial situation is complex and needs careful preparation

You need time to gather all financial documents

The 14-Day Deadline

After an emergency filing in D. Del., you have 14 days to file all remaining documents:

Schedules A/B through J (assets, liabilities, income, expenses)

Statement of Financial Affairs

Means Test calculation (Form 122A or 122C)

Tax returns for the most recent year

Pay stubs for the 60 days prior to filing

If you miss the 14-day deadline and do not receive an extension, the court will dismiss your case. This can limit the automatic stay protection in any future filing within one year.

Delaware Exemption Quick Reference

When you complete your schedules, you will need to claim exemptions to protect your property:

Homestead: $125,000

Vehicle: $15,000

Federal exemptions: Delaware allows you to choose between state and federal exemption systems

For a complete list, see the Delaware exemptions page.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file an emergency bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes. You can file an emergency (bare-bones) bankruptcy petition in Delaware to get the automatic stay protection immediately. You file only the two-page voluntary petition, a creditor list, and proof of credit counseling. You then have 14 days to file the remaining schedules and documents.

How fast can the automatic stay take effect in Delaware?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant your petition is filed with the D. Del. bankruptcy court. If you file electronically during court hours, it can happen within minutes. Emergency filings are designed for situations where a foreclosure sale, wage garnishment, or repossession is imminent.

What happens if I miss the 14-day deadline?

If you fail to file the remaining schedules and documents within 14 days, the court may dismiss your case. You can request a one-time extension, but it is not guaranteed. A dismissal could limit your ability to refile and get the automatic stay again.

Check Your Filing Eligibility

Before filing - emergency or otherwise - make sure you are eligible for a bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy and Employment in Delaware - Discrimination, Hiring, Your Rights
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/employment.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Bankruptcy and Employment in Delaware

Bankruptcy and Employment in Delaware

Federal law protects you from employment discrimination based on bankruptcy. Government employers have strict prohibitions. Private employer protections are narrower but still significant. Here is what Delaware workers need to know.

Quick answer: Under 11 U.S.C. section 525(a), government employers cannot fire, refuse to hire, or discriminate against you based on bankruptcy. Under section 525(b), private employers cannot fire you solely for filing. However, most courts hold that private employers CAN consider bankruptcy in hiring decisions. Some states add extra protections.

Federal Protections: Section 525

Section 525(a): Government Employers

Cannot terminate based on bankruptcy

Cannot refuse to hire based on bankruptcy

Cannot discriminate in any way based on bankruptcy

Covers federal, state, and local government

Also covers government-issued licenses and permits

Broad and well-established protection

Section 525(b): Private Employers

Cannot terminate solely because of bankruptcy

Hiring: Most courts say section 525(b) does NOT prohibit refusing to hire

The word "hire" was deliberately omitted from 525(b)

Cannot discriminate against current employees

Narrower than government employer protections

Some states fill the gap with credit check restrictions

Delaware-Specific Employment Protections

11 U.S.C. section 525(a) protects government employees. Delaware's Discrimination in Employment Act (Del. Code tit. 19, section 711) prohibits employment discrimination but does not specifically list bankruptcy. Delaware, as a major corporate headquarters state (over 60% of Fortune 500 companies are incorporated there), has many financial sector jobs where bankruptcy on a credit report could be a factor in hiring decisions.

Credit Checks in Employment

The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) governs employer access to credit reports:

Written consent required: An employer must get your written permission before pulling your credit report

You can say no: But declining may affect your candidacy for positions requiring financial trust

Adverse action notice: If an employer takes adverse action based on your credit report, they must notify you and provide a copy of the report

Employment versions: Employers receive a modified credit report that does not include your credit score - they see payment history, accounts, and public records (including bankruptcy)

Several states and cities restrict employer use of credit checks. Check whether Delaware has additional restrictions.

Jobs Most Affected by Bankruptcy

While most jobs are unaffected by bankruptcy, certain positions may be more sensitive:

Financial industry: Banks, investment firms, insurance companies may consider bankruptcy for positions handling money

Government with clearance: Security clearance positions are affected but not disqualified (see our security clearance page)

Law enforcement: Some agencies consider financial history

Corporate officers: C-suite positions may have different standards

Bonded positions: Jobs requiring a surety bond may be affected if the bonding company considers bankruptcy

For the vast majority of jobs - retail, healthcare, education, technology, manufacturing, service industry - bankruptcy has no effect on employment.

Professional Licenses in Delaware

Under section 525(a), Delaware licensing authorities cannot deny, revoke, or refuse to renew professional licenses solely because of bankruptcy. This includes:

Real estate licenses

Medical and nursing licenses

Legal licenses (bar admission)

Teaching certificates

Trade licenses (plumbing, electrical, etc.)

Insurance agent licenses

If a licensing board requires a "financial responsibility" showing, the underlying financial condition (not the bankruptcy filing itself) is what matters. Successfully completing bankruptcy actually demonstrates financial responsibility.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I be fired for filing bankruptcy?

Government employers: absolutely not. Private employers: cannot fire you solely for bankruptcy under section 525(b). Using it as one factor among many is a gray area.

Can an employer refuse to hire me?

Government employers cannot. Most courts hold private employers can consider bankruptcy in hiring. Some states restrict credit check use in hiring, providing indirect protection.

Can my employer check my credit?

Only with your written consent under the FCRA. You can decline, but it may affect your candidacy for financial positions.

Does bankruptcy affect professional licenses?

Government licensing authorities cannot deny or revoke licenses based on bankruptcy under section 525(a). Financial responsibility requirements may still apply.

Should I tell my employer I filed?

Generally not required unless you are in a position with financial disclosure requirements. If wage garnishment stops because of bankruptcy, your employer may become aware. Focus on your job performance.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Can I Keep My Jewelry in Delaware Bankruptcy?
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/engagement-ring.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Jewelry Exemptions

Can I Keep My Jewelry in Delaware Bankruptcy?

Your engagement ring, wedding band, and other jewelry may be protected by Delaware exemptions. Here is what the law says and how values are determined.

Quick answer: Delaware has no specific jewelry exemption in its state exemptions. However, Delaware allows filers to choose the federal exemptions, which include $1,875 for jewelry under 11 U.S.C. 522(d)(4). Most Delaware filers elect federal exemptions because they are often more generous overall.

Delaware Jewelry Exemption Details

Delaware has no specific jewelry exemption in its state exemptions. However, Delaware allows filers to choose the federal exemptions, which include $1,875 for jewelry under 11 U.S.C. 522(d)(4). Most Delaware filers elect federal exemptions because they are often more generous overall.

The exemption amount is based on the jewelry's replacement value - what a comparable item would cost at retail, not what a pawnshop or jeweler would pay you. In practice, most everyday jewelry (wedding rings, watches, earrings) falls well within the exemption limits.

How Jewelry Is Valued

Replacement value standard: The Bankruptcy Code uses replacement value - the price a retail buyer would pay for a comparable item in similar condition.

Not purchase price: What you paid is irrelevant. A ring purchased for $5,000 may have a replacement value of $2,000-$3,000.

Not resale value: Pawn shop or "we buy gold" prices are lower than replacement value and are not the correct standard.

Professional appraisal: If you have valuable jewelry, get a written appraisal from a certified appraiser. The appraiser should provide a replacement value for insurance purposes.

Trustee assessment: The Chapter 7 trustee may request an appraisal if they believe jewelry values are understated. Most trustees do not pursue jewelry worth less than $1,000-$2,000 above exemption limits.

Practical Reality: Trustees and Jewelry

In practice, bankruptcy trustees rarely pursue jewelry unless its value significantly exceeds the exemption amount. Here is why:

Transaction costs: Selling jewelry at auction or through consignment involves fees that reduce the net recovery for creditors.

Low recovery rates: Used jewelry typically sells for 20-40% of retail replacement value at liquidation.

De minimis recovery: If the net benefit to creditors is less than a few hundred dollars, most trustees determine the sale is not worth the effort.

Emotional factor: Trustees understand that taking wedding rings creates bad optics and may exercise discretion.

Important: Always disclose all jewelry honestly on your schedules. Hiding assets is fraud. If your jewelry is within the exemption, the trustee will not take it. If it is close to the limit, the trustee will likely not pursue it.

Protecting Jewelry That Exceeds the Exemption

If your jewelry exceeds Delaware's exemption, you may have options:

Wildcard exemption: If Delaware offers a wildcard (general property) exemption, you can stack it on top of the jewelry exemption to protect additional value.

Chapter 13: In Chapter 13, you keep all your property. Instead of surrendering jewelry, you pay creditors the equivalent value through your plan. This is often the better option for people with valuable jewelry.

Negotiate with trustee: You may be able to pay the trustee the non-exempt value in cash to keep the jewelry - called "buying back" the asset from the estate.

Federal exemptions: If Delaware allows the federal exemptions (11 U.S.C. 522(d)), you may get a higher jewelry exemption ($1,875) plus a significant wildcard ($1,475 + up to $13,950 of unused homestead).

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my engagement ring in Delaware bankruptcy?

It depends on the ring's value and Delaware's exemptions. No specific jewelry exemption - use federal $1,875 (11 U.S.C. 522(d)(4)). If your ring's value is below the exemption limit, you can keep it. If it exceeds the limit, you may need to use additional exemptions (like a wildcard) to protect it.

What is the jewelry exemption in Delaware?

No specific jewelry exemption - use federal $1,875 (11 U.S.C. 522(d)(4)). This applies to all jewelry including engagement rings, wedding bands, watches, and other personal adornment. Values are based on resale/liquidation value, not what you paid.

How is jewelry valued in bankruptcy?

Jewelry is valued at replacement value - what it would cost to buy a comparable item at retail, not what a jeweler would pay you. In practice, bankruptcy trustees often accept values significantly below retail. A professional appraisal can help establish the value.

What if my jewelry is worth more than the exemption?

If your jewelry exceeds the exemption limit, you may be able to protect the excess using a wildcard exemption (if available in your state). Otherwise, the trustee may seek to sell the jewelry and return the exempt portion to you. In practice, trustees rarely pursue jewelry unless the value significantly exceeds exemptions.

Do I have to disclose all jewelry in bankruptcy?

Yes. All assets must be disclosed on Schedule A/B, including jewelry. Failure to disclose assets is bankruptcy fraud and can result in denial of discharge, fines, or criminal prosecution. Always disclose - even if you believe the item is fully exempt.

What about inherited jewelry or family heirlooms?

Inherited jewelry and heirlooms are valued the same way as any other jewelry - replacement value. Sentimental value does not factor into the financial analysis. However, trustees rarely pursue items with modest resale value, even if sentimental value is high.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment

## Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction in Delaware?
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/eviction.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Eviction

Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction in Delaware?

Timing is everything. Bankruptcy can halt an eviction if you file before a judgment for possession is entered. After a judgment, the protection is limited.

Quick answer: If no eviction judgment has been entered, filing bankruptcy immediately stops the eviction through the automatic stay. If a judgment already exists, section 362(b)(22) allows the landlord to proceed. File before the judgment to maximize protection.

Before vs. After Eviction Judgment

Before Judgment

Automatic stay stops eviction

Landlord cannot proceed in state court

Chapter 13 can cure back rent over 3-5 years

You must pay post-petition rent going forward

Emergency filing possible in hours

After Judgment

Section 362(b)(22) limits protection

Landlord can proceed with eviction

Exception: if state law allows cure after judgment, you may file certification under 362(l)

Most Delaware courts apply this strictly

Chapter 7 will discharge the rent debt but not stop eviction

Delaware Eviction Timeline

Governing law: 25 Del. C. Chapter 55

Delaware requires a 5-day notice for nonpayment. Summary possession actions typically take 2-3 weeks to reach a hearing in Justice of the Peace Court.

Understanding this timeline is critical for deciding when to file bankruptcy. Once the landlord obtains a judgment for possession, your bankruptcy protections are significantly reduced.

Key window: File bankruptcy BEFORE the judge enters a judgment for possession. Once a judgment is entered, section 362(b)(22) may allow the eviction to continue despite your filing.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Evictions

Chapter 7

Discharges back rent (pre-petition)

Automatic stay is temporary (case lasts 3-4 months)

Does not allow you to cure arrears

Best if you plan to move anyway

Eliminates personal liability for lease balance

Chapter 13

Allows curing back rent through plan

Automatic stay lasts 3-5 years (plan duration)

You must assume the lease and stay current

Best if you want to keep your housing

Plan payments include arrears catch-up

Emergency Filing to Stop Eviction

If eviction is imminent, an emergency bankruptcy filing can be done quickly:

Bare-bones petition: File just the petition, Statement of Social Security Number, and credit counseling certificate. You have 14 days to file the remaining schedules.

Immediate protection: The automatic stay takes effect the moment the petition is filed with the bankruptcy court - not when the landlord is notified.

Proof of filing: Get a copy of the filed petition with the case number and give it to your landlord and the state court handling the eviction.

Filing fee: Chapter 7 costs $338 and Chapter 13 costs $313. Fee waivers are available for those below 150% of the poverty line.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop an eviction in Delaware?

It depends on timing. If no eviction judgment has been entered, filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately halts eviction proceedings. If a judgment for possession already exists, section 362(b)(22) limits the stay's protection, and the landlord may be able to proceed.

What is section 362(b)(22)?

Section 362(b)(22) of the Bankruptcy Code is an exception to the automatic stay. If a landlord obtained a judgment for possession before you filed bankruptcy, the stay does not prevent the landlord from continuing the eviction. This was added by the 2005 BAPCPA amendments to prevent abuse of bankruptcy to delay evictions.

What if I file bankruptcy before the eviction judgment in Delaware?

If no judgment for possession has been entered, the automatic stay stops the eviction cold. The landlord cannot proceed with the eviction, change locks, or remove your property. However, you must still pay post-petition rent - the stay does not excuse future rent payments.

Can I cure a lease default in Chapter 13?

In Chapter 13, you can cure lease defaults (back rent) through your repayment plan. You assume the lease and pay arrears over the plan period (3-5 years). This only works if your landlord has not yet obtained a judgment for possession.

Does filing bankruptcy erase back rent?

Chapter 7 can discharge back rent owed before filing, but it does not stop an eviction if a judgment already exists. Chapter 13 lets you cure arrears while keeping your home. Post-petition rent must always be paid regardless of the chapter filed.

How quickly can I file bankruptcy to stop an eviction in Delaware?

Emergency (bare-bones) bankruptcy filings can be done in hours. You file the petition with minimal schedules and have 14 days to complete the rest. The automatic stay takes effect the moment the petition is filed with the court.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House

## Delaware Bankruptcy Exemptions - What You Can Keep
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/exemptions.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Exemptions

Delaware Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you get to keep when you file bankruptcy. Understanding Delaware's exemptions is critical - they can mean the difference between keeping and losing your home, car, and savings.

What Are Exemptions?

Bankruptcy exemptions are laws that protect certain property from being taken to pay your creditors. In Chapter 7, non-exempt property can be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, the value of non-exempt property affects how much you must pay through your plan.

Delaware allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions. Many filers compare both to determine which provides better protection for their specific assets.

Delaware State Exemptions - Full List

Homestead - $125,000

Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 of equity in your primary residence. Delaware also allows filers to choose federal exemptions, which may be more beneficial for renters.

Del. Code tit. 10 sec. 4914

Motor Vehicle - No specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal alternative: $4,450)

Protects equity in one motor vehicle up to the exemption limit. If you owe more than the car is worth (you are "underwater"), there is no equity to protect and the exemption is not needed.

Other Delaware Exemptions

PropertyAmount

Household goods, furnishingsNo specific limit under state law

ClothingNo specific limit under state law

Tools of trade$15,000 (tools of trade)

JewelryNo specific exemption

Wildcard$500 (state); federal wildcard: $1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement accounts (IRA, 401k, pension)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Workers' compensationFully exempt

Public assistance benefitsFully exempt

Federal Exemption Alternative

PropertyFederal Exemption

Homestead$27,900

Vehicle$4,450

Household goods$700 per item / $14,875 total

Wildcard$1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Tools of trade$2,800

Retirement accountsFully exempt

Special Notes

Delaware allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions. Many Delaware filers choose federal exemptions, especially renters who benefit from the larger federal wildcard.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Delaware homestead exemption?

Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 of equity in your primary residence. Delaware also allows filers to choose federal exemptions, which may be more beneficial for renters.

How much vehicle equity can I protect in Delaware?

Delaware's motor vehicle exemption is No specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal alternative: $4,450). If your car equity is within that limit, you keep it in Chapter 7.

Does Delaware allow federal exemptions?

Yes. Delaware allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions.

## Delaware Bankruptcy FAQ - 15 Common Questions Answered
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/faq.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Faq

Delaware Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the 15 most common questions from Delaware residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Eligibility

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Delaware?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,200 to $2,000 for Chapter 7 and $3,000 to $4,500 for Chapter 13. Credit counseling costs about $25-50 total. Chapter 13 fees can be paid through your repayment plan. Fee waivers available for low-income Chapter 7 filers.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Delaware?

Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 of equity in your home. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan. Most homeowners keep their homes in bankruptcy.

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy in Delaware?

Delaware protects No specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal alternative: $4,450) in vehicle equity. If your car equity is within the exemption limit, you keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments through your plan.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting of creditors happens about 30 days after filing.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay takes effect the moment your petition is filed and immediately stops wage garnishments, bank levies, lawsuits, foreclosures, and repossessions.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster (3-4 months) and eliminates most unsecured debt, but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 lets you keep all property and catch up on secured debt arrears, but requires 3-5 years of payments. Your income, assets, and goals determine the best choice.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Delaware?

Delaware has 1 district: D. Del.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Yes. Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years and Chapter 13 for 7 years. However, many filers see credit scores start recovering within 1-2 years as debt-to-income ratios improve.

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical bills are unsecured debts that are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. Medical debt is one of the most common reasons people file bankruptcy.

Will bankruptcy eliminate my student loans?

Generally no. Student loans are not dischargeable except in cases of undue hardship, which requires a separate adversary proceeding and a difficult legal standard.

What is the means test?

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. It compares your household income to the Delaware median. If below, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed expense calculation determines eligibility.

Can creditors still call me after I file?

No. The automatic stay prohibits all collection activity, including phone calls, letters, and emails. Creditors who violate the stay can be held in contempt and ordered to pay damages.

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

A brief hearing about 30 days after filing where the bankruptcy trustee asks you questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Can I file bankruptcy more than once?

Yes, but there are time limits. After a Chapter 7 discharge, you must wait 8 years for another Chapter 7 or 4 years for Chapter 13. After a Chapter 13 discharge, wait 6 years for Chapter 7 or 2 years for another Chapter 13.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay is a court order that takes effect immediately when you file bankruptcy. It stops lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, repossessions, and collection calls.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test

File Again?
How to File

## How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/find-bankruptcy-filing.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Find Filings

How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Delaware

Bankruptcy filings are public records. Here is how to search them in Delaware - from free options to the official PACER system.

Quick answer: Search PACER at pacer.uscourts.gov ($0.10/page, $3 cap per doc). For free access, use CourtListener.com (RECAP archive) or call the District of Delaware clerk's office. The Delaware bankruptcy court website is https://www.deb.uscourts.gov.

Option 1: PACER (Official System)

PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) is the federal court system's official electronic records platform.

Website: pacer.uscourts.gov

Cost: $0.10 per page, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly charges under $30 are waived.

Search by: Name, case number, Social Security number (last 4), date range

Delaware court: https://ecf.deb.uscourts.gov

Registration: Free to create an account. Takes about 5 minutes.

1

Register

Create a free PACER account at pacer.uscourts.gov

2

Search

Use the national case locator or go directly to the Delaware bankruptcy court

3

View

View docket entries and download documents ($0.10/page)

Option 2: RECAP / CourtListener (Free)

RECAP is a free browser extension that contributes PACER documents to a public archive. CourtListener hosts this archive and makes it searchable.

Website: courtlistener.com

Cost: Completely free

Coverage: Millions of documents, growing daily. Not every document is available, but many are.

Browser extension: Install RECAP for Chrome or Firefox. When you view PACER documents, RECAP automatically saves a copy to the public archive.

Option 3: Court Clerk's Office

You can contact the District of Delaware bankruptcy court clerk's office directly:

Phone: Call the clerk and request a case search by name or case number. Basic case information (filing date, chapter, status) is typically provided for free.

In person: Visit the courthouse and use the public access terminals. No PACER account needed.

Website: https://www.deb.uscourts.gov may have a free case search or CM/ECF lookup

What Information Is Available

Docket sheet: Every document filed in the case, with dates and descriptions

Petition: Debtor's name, address, chapter filed, attorney

Schedules: Assets, liabilities, income, expenses (with sensitive info redacted)

Claims register: Every creditor claim filed in the case

Orders: Court orders, discharge, dismissal

Meeting of creditors (341): Date and outcome

Sensitive information is redacted: full Social Security numbers, complete financial account numbers, and the names of minor children are not visible in public filings.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I find bankruptcy filings in Delaware?

Bankruptcy filings in Delaware are public records available through PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) at pacer.uscourts.gov. You can search by name, case number, or Social Security number. The District of Delaware has its electronic filing system at https://ecf.deb.uscourts.gov.

Is PACER free?

PACER charges $0.10 per page viewed, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly fees under $30 are waived. You can also use RECAP (a free browser extension) to access previously downloaded documents at no cost via CourtListener.com.

Can I search bankruptcy filings for free?

Yes. CourtListener.com (via the RECAP archive) has millions of free bankruptcy documents. Many court websites also offer free case information lookups. The bankruptcy court clerk's office will search records for free over the phone for basic information.

Which courts handle bankruptcy in Delaware?

Bankruptcy cases in Delaware are filed in the District of Delaware. The court website is https://www.deb.uscourts.gov. PACER electronic filing is at https://ecf.deb.uscourts.gov.

How long are bankruptcy records available?

Bankruptcy records on PACER are generally available for the life of the case plus several years. Older cases may be archived but still accessible. Bankruptcy stays on your credit report for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7) from the filing date.

Can I see someone else's bankruptcy filing?

Yes. Bankruptcy filings are public records. Anyone can search PACER or visit the court clerk to view case documents. Some sensitive information (Social Security numbers, financial account numbers) is redacted from public filings.

## Foreclosure and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/foreclosure.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Foreclosure

Stop Foreclosure with Bankruptcy in Delaware

How bankruptcy stops foreclosure in Delaware. Delaware uses judicial foreclosure (approximately 6 months or longer). Chapter 13 mortgage catch-up, Chapter 7 timeline, and your options.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Foreclosure in Delaware

Type: Judicial foreclosure

Typical timeline: approximately 6 months or longer

Judicial foreclosure requires the lender to file a lawsuit, which gives you more time to prepare. However, once a judgment is entered, the sale can proceed quickly.

How Bankruptcy Stops Foreclosure

Chapter 7

Immediate stop: Automatic stay halts foreclosure

Duration: Stay lasts 3-4 months (until case closes)

After case: Lender can resume foreclosure

Best for: Buying time or surrendering the home

Deficiency: Mortgage deficiency is discharged

Chapter 13

Immediate stop: Automatic stay halts foreclosure

Duration: Stay lasts 3-5 years (entire plan)

Arrears cure: Catch up on missed payments through plan

Best for: Saving your home long-term

Lien stripping: May eliminate junior liens

Chapter 13 Mortgage Cure

How It Works

If you are $12,000 behind on your mortgage, Chapter 13 lets you spread that $12,000 over your 3-5 year plan. On a 60-month plan, that is $200 per month in arrears payments on top of your regular mortgage payment. During the plan, the lender cannot foreclose as long as you make both payments.

Requirements

You must have regular income sufficient to fund the plan

Current mortgage payments must be made on time during the plan

Arrears payments must be completed within the plan period

You must complete all plan payments to receive a discharge

Save Your Home Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop foreclosure in Delaware?

Yes. Filing either Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 immediately triggers the automatic stay, which halts all foreclosure proceedings. Delaware uses judicial foreclosure, which typically takes approximately 6 months or longer.

How does foreclosure work in Delaware?

Delaware uses judicial foreclosure. This means the lender must file a lawsuit in court to foreclose, which generally takes longer and gives you more time. The typical timeline is approximately 6 months or longer.

Is Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 better for stopping foreclosure?

Chapter 13 is generally better for saving your home because it allows you to catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years. Chapter 7 only delays foreclosure temporarily - once the case is closed (3-4 months), the lender can resume foreclosure proceedings.

How does Chapter 13 cure mortgage arrears?

Chapter 13 lets you catch up on missed mortgage payments (arrears) through your 3-5 year repayment plan while making current mortgage payments going forward. The total arrears are spread over the plan period, making catch-up affordable.

What if I cannot afford my mortgage?

If your mortgage is unaffordable, you may be able to negotiate a loan modification during your bankruptcy case. If the home is not worth saving, you can surrender it in Chapter 7 and discharge the mortgage deficiency, or in Chapter 13 you can surrender and have the deficiency treated as unsecured debt.

What is the homestead exemption in Delaware?

Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 in home equity. If your equity is within the exemption, your home is protected from the bankruptcy trustee in Chapter 7.

## Delaware Bankruptcy Glossary
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/glossary.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Glossary

Delaware Bankruptcy Glossary

Key bankruptcy terms and definitions for Delaware filers, from automatic stay to wildcard exemption.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Terms A-Z

341 Meeting (Meeting of Creditors)
A required hearing where the bankruptcy trustee (and any creditors who choose to attend) asks you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and petition. Held about 30 days after filing in D. Del..

Adversary Proceeding
A lawsuit within a bankruptcy case, often filed to challenge the dischargeability of a specific debt or to recover fraudulent transfers.

Automatic Stay
A court order that takes effect immediately upon filing, stopping most collection actions, lawsuits, foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments. See 11 U.S.C. section 362.

Chapter 7
Liquidation bankruptcy that eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. A trustee may sell non-exempt assets to pay creditors. Requires passing the means test.

Chapter 13
Reorganization bankruptcy for individuals with regular income. Debtor proposes a 3-5 year repayment plan to catch up on secured debts and pay a portion of unsecured debts.

Cramdown
Reducing the amount owed on a secured debt to the current value of the collateral. Available in Chapter 13 for vehicles purchased more than 910 days before filing and other secured debts.

Credit Counseling
A required course from an approved provider that must be completed within 180 days before filing bankruptcy. A second course (debtor education) is required before discharge.

Creditor
Any person or entity to whom you owe money. Includes secured creditors (backed by collateral), unsecured creditors (no collateral), and priority creditors (taxes, support obligations).

Discharge
A court order that permanently eliminates your personal liability for dischargeable debts. The discharge injunction (section 524) prevents creditors from collecting on discharged debts forever.

Disposable Income
Income remaining after subtracting allowed expenses. In Chapter 13, disposable income determines your plan payment amount.

Exemptions
Laws that protect specific property from being taken by the trustee in Chapter 7 or reduce what you pay in Chapter 13. Delaware homestead exemption: $125,000; vehicle: $15,000.

Fraudulent Transfer
A transfer of property made to hinder, delay, or defraud creditors. The trustee can recover such transfers made within 2 years before filing (or longer under state law).

Lien
A legal claim on property as security for a debt. Common types include mortgage liens, auto liens, tax liens, and judgment liens.

Lien Stripping
Removing a wholly unsecured junior mortgage in Chapter 13. If the home value is less than the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage can be treated as unsecured debt.

Means Test
A formula under section 707(b) that determines Chapter 7 eligibility based on income and expenses. Compares your income to Delaware median income for your household size.

Motion for Relief from Stay
A request by a creditor asking the court to lift the automatic stay, usually to allow foreclosure or repossession to proceed.

Non-Dischargeable Debt
Debts that survive bankruptcy, including student loans (absent undue hardship), most taxes, child support, alimony, debts from fraud, and DUI-related debts.

Petition
The official document filed with the bankruptcy court to start a case. Includes your personal information, chapter selected, and basic financial data.

Plan (Chapter 13)
A proposal showing how you will repay creditors over 3-5 years. Must pay priority debts in full, cure arrears on secured debts, and pay unsecured creditors at least what they would receive in Chapter 7.

Priority Debt
Debts that must be paid in full in Chapter 13 and cannot be discharged in Chapter 7. Includes recent taxes, child support, alimony, and certain government fines.

Proof of Claim
A form filed by a creditor establishing the amount and type of debt owed. Required for the creditor to receive payment in the case.

Reaffirmation Agreement
A voluntary agreement to remain liable for a debt after bankruptcy, typically used to keep a car or other secured property. Must be filed with the court.

Schedules
Detailed forms listing all assets, debts, income, expenses, and recent financial transactions. Required within 14 days of filing.

Secured Debt
A debt backed by collateral (like a mortgage or car loan). The creditor can repossess the collateral if the debt is not paid.

Trustee
A court-appointed official who administers your bankruptcy case. Chapter 7 trustees liquidate non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 trustees collect and distribute plan payments.

Unsecured Debt
A debt not backed by collateral, such as credit cards, medical bills, and personal loans. Generally dischargeable in bankruptcy.

Wildcard Exemption
An exemption that can be applied to any property type. Available under both state and federal exemption systems in Delaware.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months through liquidation of non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 is a 3-5 year repayment plan for people with regular income. Chapter 7 has income limits (means test); Chapter 13 has debt limits ($2,750,000).

What does discharge mean in bankruptcy?

A discharge is a permanent court order that eliminates your personal liability for specified debts. After discharge, creditors can never collect on those debts again. Not all debts are dischargeable - student loans, most taxes, child support, and alimony typically survive.

Learn More About Bankruptcy in Delaware

Chapter 7 Guide
Chapter 13 Guide

## Bankruptcy Income Limits in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/income-limits.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Income Limits

Bankruptcy Income Limits in Delaware

Your income determines which bankruptcy chapter you qualify for in Delaware and how much you pay in Chapter 13. Here is how the income limits work.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Chapter 7 Income Limits

Chapter 7 uses the means test to determine eligibility. The test compares your household's current monthly income (averaged over the prior 6 months) to the Delaware median income for your household size.

If Below Median Income

You automatically pass the means test and qualify for Chapter 7. No further calculation is needed.

If Above Median Income

You must complete the full means test, which deducts allowed expenses from your income. If the remaining disposable income falls below the threshold, you still qualify. The means test uses IRS expense standards plus your actual secured debt payments.

Delaware median income figures are updated twice per year (April and October) by the U.S. Trustee Program. Check bankruptcymeanstest.org for current figures.

Chapter 13 - No Income Ceiling

Chapter 13 has no maximum income limit. Anyone with regular income can file, regardless of how high that income is. However, your income affects:

Plan length: Above-median income requires a 5-year plan; below-median allows 3-5 years

Payment amount: Higher income means higher monthly plan payments

Debt limits: Total debts (secured plus unsecured) cannot exceed $2,750,000

Chapter 13 requires regular income - wages, salary, self-employment, Social Security, pension, or other consistent sources.

Income Calculation Details

Current monthly income (CMI) includes almost all income from all sources over the 6 months before filing:

Wages, salary, overtime, bonuses, commissions

Self-employment income (gross receipts minus ordinary business expenses)

Interest, dividends, rental income

Pension, retirement, annuity payments

Unemployment compensation

Contributions from household members (spouse, non-filing partner)

Excluded from CMI: Social Security benefits, payments to victims of war crimes or terrorism.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the income limits for bankruptcy in Delaware?

For Chapter 7, your household income must be at or below the Delaware median for your household size, or you must pass the means test expense deduction. Chapter 13 has no income ceiling - anyone with regular income can file. However, Chapter 13 has a debt limit of $2,750,000.

What if my income is too high for Chapter 7?

If your income exceeds the Delaware median, you still might qualify through the means test expense deductions. If not, Chapter 13 allows any income level - higher income just means higher plan payments over 3-5 years.

Does household size affect income limits?

Yes. The median income threshold increases with household size. A single filer has a lower threshold than a family of four. Household size typically includes everyone living in the home and dependents you support.

Check Your Eligibility

The means test and discharge screener can help determine your options.

Means Test Guide
Discharge Screener

## Delaware Bankruptcy - Chapter 7 & 13 Filing Guide [2026]
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/

Skip to main content

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP Rule 9037 PII Scanner

Delaware Bankruptcy - Your Complete Filing Guide for 2026

You are not alone. Delaware's consumer bankruptcy filing rate is moderate. The district is more widely known for its large corporate Chapter 11 cases. This free guide covers everything Delaware residents need to know about filing bankruptcy, from exemptions to costs to what happens after your case is filed.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

$125,000

Homestead Exemption

1

Federal District

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession. No means test required.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

Delaware Federal Court District

Delaware has 1 federal bankruptcy district. Where you live determines where you file.

District of Delaware (D. Del.)

over 15,000 cases

Courthouse: 824 N. Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801

Phone: (302) 252-2900 | deb.uscourts.gov

Delaware is also a major venue for corporate Chapter 11 filings due to its business-friendly legal environment.

Delaware Exemptions at a Glance

Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 of equity in your primary residence. Delaware also allows filers to choose federal exemptions, which may be more beneficial for renters.

Delaware allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions, which can be advantageous depending on your situation.

PropertyDelaware Exemption

Homestead$125,000

VehicleNo specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal alternative: $4,450)

Household goodsNo specific limit under state law

Wildcard$500 (state); federal wildcard: $1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Full exemption details →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Delaware?

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,200 to $2,000 for Chapter 7; $3,000 to $4,500 for Chapter 13 in Delaware

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Delaware?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Delaware typically range from $1,200 to $2,000 for Chapter 7 and $3,000 to $4,500 for Chapter 13. You will also need to pay for two required credit counseling courses, usually $25-$50 total. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Delaware?

Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 of equity in your home. If your equity is below that amount, you can keep your home in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments through your repayment plan. See all exemptions.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan before receiving a discharge. The 341 meeting of creditors usually happens about 30 days after filing.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt quickly but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 allows you to keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Your income, assets, and type of debt determine which chapter is best. Compare chapters.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Delaware?

Delaware has 1 federal bankruptcy district: D. Del.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

See all frequently asked questions →

## Judgments and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/judgments.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Judgments

Judgments and Bankruptcy in Delaware

A civil judgment against you does not mean you are out of options. Bankruptcy can discharge the underlying debt and even remove judgment liens from your property in Delaware.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Discharging Judgments in Bankruptcy

Whether a judgment is dischargeable depends on the type of debt behind it:

Generally Dischargeable

Credit card judgments

Medical debt judgments

Personal loan judgments

Breach of contract judgments

Deficiency judgments (auto, mortgage)

Generally NOT Dischargeable

Judgments based on fraud

Judgments for willful injury

DUI/DWI damage judgments

Domestic support judgments

Student loan judgments (absent hardship)

Government fine/penalty judgments

Removing Judgment Liens Under Section 522(f)

Even when a judgment is discharged, the lien it created on your property may survive unless you take action. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can file a motion to avoid (remove) a judicial lien if it impairs your exemptions.

How It Works in Delaware

Home: If your home equity minus the judgment lien and any mortgages is less than the $125,000 homestead exemption, the lien impairs your exemption and can be avoided

Vehicle: Same analysis using the $15,000 vehicle exemption

Other property: Any exempt property with an impairing judgment lien qualifies

You must file a separate motion with the D. Del. court to avoid the lien. The discharge alone does not remove it.

Stopping Judgment Enforcement

The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately stops all judgment enforcement:

Wage garnishment must stop

Bank account levies are frozen

Property liens cannot be enforced

No new collection lawsuits can be filed

Existing lawsuits are paused

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge a judgment in bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes. Most money judgments based on unsecured debt (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) are dischargeable in bankruptcy in Delaware. The underlying debt determines dischargeability - if the original debt was dischargeable, the judgment based on that debt is also dischargeable.

Can I remove a judgment lien from my property?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can avoid (remove) a judicial lien on your property to the extent it impairs your bankruptcy exemptions. In Delaware, this is commonly used to remove judgment liens from homes (homestead exemption: $125,000) and vehicles (exemption: $15,000).

Does the judgment creditor have to stop collecting?

Yes. The automatic stay stops all collection on judgments the instant you file. The creditor cannot garnish wages, levy bank accounts, or enforce the judgment in any way while the stay is in effect.

Facing a Judgment?

Find out if bankruptcy can eliminate your judgment debt.

Discharge Screener
Delaware Exemptions

## Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/keep-your-car.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Keep Your Car

Keeping Your Car in Delaware Bankruptcy

How to keep your car when filing bankruptcy in Delaware. Vehicle exemption ($15,000), reaffirmation, redemption, and Chapter 13 cramdown options.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Delaware Vehicle Exemption

Exemption amount: $15,000 per debtor

How equity works: If your car is worth $12,000 and you owe $10,000, your equity is $2,000. If that is within Delaware's vehicle exemption, the car is protected.

Delaware allows you to choose between state and federal exemptions, so compare both to see which protects more of your vehicle equity.

Options in Chapter 7

Reaffirmation

Sign a reaffirmation agreement to keep paying your car loan. You keep the car and the debt survives the bankruptcy. Monthly payments continue as before. Your attorney must certify it does not impose undue hardship.

Redemption

Pay the current fair market value of the car in a lump sum, regardless of how much you owe. If you owe $18,000 on a car worth $10,000, you pay $10,000 and keep the car. Some lenders specialize in redemption financing.

Surrender

If you no longer want or need the car, you can surrender it. The remaining loan balance is discharged along with your other debts. This may make sense if the car is worth much less than what you owe.

Options in Chapter 13

Keep the Car and Catch Up

Chapter 13 lets you keep your car while catching up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan. The automatic stay stops repossession the moment you file.

Cramdown (910-Day Rule)

If you purchased your car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing, you can reduce the loan balance to the car's current market value under 11 U.S.C. section 1325(a). Example: if you owe $20,000 on a car worth $12,000, you pay $12,000 through your plan at a court-approved interest rate. The remaining $8,000 is treated as unsecured debt.

The 910-day rule: If you purchased the car within 910 days of filing, you must pay the full loan balance - no cramdown is available.

Car in Bankruptcy Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes, in most cases. Delaware's vehicle exemption protects up to $15,000 in equity per vehicle. Delaware allows you to choose between state and federal exemptions, so compare both to see which protects more of your vehicle equity. If your equity is within the exemption, you can keep your car in Chapter 7. Chapter 13 lets you keep your car while catching up on payments.

What is the vehicle exemption in Delaware?

Delaware's vehicle exemption is $15,000 per debtor. Equity is the difference between your car's current value and what you owe on it. If your equity is below the exemption amount, the car is fully protected.

What is reaffirmation in bankruptcy?

Reaffirmation is an agreement to keep paying your car loan as if the bankruptcy never happened. You keep the car but remain personally liable for the debt. Your attorney must certify it does not impose undue hardship, and the court must approve it.

What is redemption in Chapter 7?

Redemption lets you pay the current market value of the vehicle in a single lump sum, regardless of how much you owe. If you owe $15,000 on a car worth $8,000, you can keep the car by paying $8,000. The challenge is coming up with the lump sum.

What is a Chapter 13 cramdown for a car?

If you purchased your car more than 910 days before filing Chapter 13, you can reduce the loan balance to the car's current market value. You pay this reduced amount through your 3-5 year plan at a court-approved interest rate. This is called a cramdown under 11 U.S.C. section 1325(a).

What happens to my car loan in bankruptcy in Delaware?

It depends on the chapter and your choices. In Chapter 7, you can reaffirm (keep paying), redeem (pay current value), or surrender the car. In Chapter 13, you can keep the car and catch up on missed payments or cramdown the loan balance.

## Keep Your House in Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/keep-your-house.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Keep Your House

Keeping Your House in Delaware Bankruptcy

How to keep your home when filing bankruptcy in Delaware. Homestead exemption ($125,000), Chapter 13 mortgage catch-up, and foreclosure defense.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Delaware Homestead Exemption

Exemption amount: $125,000

How equity works: If your home is worth $300,000 and you owe $250,000, your equity is $50,000. If that is within Delaware's homestead exemption, your home is fully protected in Chapter 7.

Delaware allows you to choose between state and federal exemptions.

Chapter 7 - Keeping Your Home

In Chapter 7, you can keep your home if your equity is within the homestead exemption. You must continue making mortgage payments - Chapter 7 does not eliminate mortgage liens. The trustee examines whether there is non-exempt equity that could be used to pay creditors.

Equity within exemption: You keep the home and continue payments

Equity exceeds exemption: The trustee may sell the home (rare if equity is modest)

Underwater home: No equity risk - you keep the home as long as you pay

Chapter 13 - Mortgage Catch-Up

Stopping Foreclosure

Filing Chapter 13 immediately triggers the automatic stay, which halts all foreclosure proceedings. Delaware uses judicial foreclosure (typically approximately 6 months or longer), so timing your filing is critical.

Curing Mortgage Arrears

Chapter 13 lets you catch up on missed mortgage payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan while making current payments going forward. This is one of the most powerful tools for saving your home.

Lien Stripping

If your home's value is less than what you owe on the first mortgage, a second mortgage or HELOC can be stripped (reclassified as unsecured debt) through your Chapter 13 plan. The second lienholder receives only a fraction of what is owed, and the lien is removed upon plan completion.

Home in Bankruptcy Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my house if I file bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes, in most cases. Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 in home equity. If your equity is within the exemption, your home is protected in Chapter 7. Chapter 13 lets you catch up on missed mortgage payments over 3-5 years.

What is Delaware's homestead exemption?

Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 in equity in your primary residence. Delaware allows you to choose between state and federal exemptions. Equity is your home's current value minus what you owe on it.

Can Chapter 13 stop foreclosure?

Yes. Filing Chapter 13 immediately stops foreclosure through the automatic stay. You then catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year repayment plan while making current mortgage payments going forward. Delaware uses judicial foreclosure, which typically takes approximately 6 months or longer.

What happens to my mortgage in Chapter 7?

Chapter 7 does not eliminate your mortgage lien. If you want to keep the home, you continue making payments. If your equity exceeds the homestead exemption, the trustee may sell the home. Chapter 7 does eliminate personal liability for the mortgage debt if you choose to surrender.

Can I strip a second mortgage in Chapter 13?

If your home is worth less than what you owe on the first mortgage, a second mortgage or HELOC may be stripped (reclassified as unsecured debt) in Chapter 13. This is called lien stripping and is available in the District of Delaware.

How does judicial foreclosure work in Delaware?

Delaware uses judicial foreclosure, which typically takes approximately 6 months or longer. This is the timeline you are working against if you need to file bankruptcy to stop a foreclosure sale.

## Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Delaware - Discharge, Alternatives, Protections
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/medical-debt-only.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Delaware

Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Delaware

Medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy in America. It is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. You do not need to feel guilty about discharging medical debt - the system is designed for exactly this situation.

Quick answer: Medical debt is 100% dischargeable in bankruptcy. You cannot file only for medical debt (all eligible debts are included), but medical bills are unsecured, non-priority debt - the easiest category to discharge. Before filing, check if you qualify for hospital charity care or financial assistance programs in Delaware.

Medical Debt in Bankruptcy: The Basics

Medical debt is classified as general unsecured, non-priority debt in bankruptcy - the same category as credit cards and personal loans. This is the most favorable classification for discharge:

Chapter 7: Medical debt is completely eliminated when you receive your discharge (typically 3-4 months after filing)

Chapter 13: Medical debt is paid at whatever percentage your plan pays to unsecured creditors. In many Chapter 13 plans, unsecured creditors receive 10% or less. A $50,000 medical bill might result in paying $5,000 or less over 3-5 years.

No exception to discharge: Unlike student loans or certain tax debts, there is no exception that prevents medical debt from being discharged

Delaware Medical Debt Protections

Medical debt is fully dischargeable in Delaware bankruptcy. Delaware does not have a specific medical debt protection statute comparable to some states. Delaware hospitals receiving Hill-Burton funds must provide charity care. The Delaware Health Care Commission oversees healthcare costs. Medical debt from out-of-state providers (common given Delaware's small size and proximity to Philadelphia/Baltimore) is treated the same in bankruptcy.

Before Filing: Exhaust Other Options

Hospital Financial Assistance (Charity Care)

Under the ACA, nonprofit hospitals are required to have financial assistance policies. Many hospitals will significantly reduce or eliminate bills for patients below certain income thresholds (typically 200-400% of the federal poverty level). Ask the billing department about financial assistance before paying or filing bankruptcy.

Negotiate the Bill

Medical bills are often negotiable. Strategies include:

Request an itemized bill and dispute any errors

Ask for the "self-pay" or "uninsured" rate (often 40-60% less than the billed amount)

Offer a lump-sum settlement for less than the full amount

Request a payment plan with no interest

When Bankruptcy Makes More Sense

Bankruptcy is often the better choice when:

Medical debt is combined with other unsecured debts (credit cards, personal loans)

Total debt exceeds your ability to repay within a reasonable time

Creditors are suing or garnishing wages

The hospital denies charity care or the remaining balance is still unaffordable

Medical debt is on top of existing financial stress

Credit Report Changes for Medical Debt

Recent changes to credit reporting have reduced the impact of medical debt:

Medical debt under $500: No longer reported by Equifax, Experian, or TransUnion (as of 2023)

Paid medical collections: Removed from credit reports

1-year waiting period: Medical debt cannot appear on credit reports until at least 1 year after going to collections

FICO 10T and VantageScore 4.0: Newer scoring models reduce the weight of medical collections

Despite these improvements, large medical debts sent to third-party collectors can still damage your credit. Bankruptcy provides a definitive resolution.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file bankruptcy just for medical debt?

You cannot selectively discharge only medical debt - all eligible unsecured debts are included. But if medical debt is your primary problem, bankruptcy eliminates it along with other unsecured debts.

Is medical debt dischargeable?

Yes, 100%. Medical debt is unsecured, non-priority debt - the easiest category to discharge. There is no exception preventing discharge.

Should I negotiate or file bankruptcy?

If medical debt is your only debt, try negotiation and charity care first. If you have multiple debts or the amounts are unmanageable, bankruptcy may be more efficient.

Will medical debt affect my credit?

Medical debt under $500 is no longer reported. Paid medical collections are removed. Large unpaid amounts can still affect credit until resolved through payment, settlement, or bankruptcy.

Does Delaware have medical debt protections?

Protections vary. Check with your hospital about financial assistance programs. Nonprofit hospitals are required to have charity care policies under federal law.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/medical-debt.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Medical Debt

Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in Delaware

How bankruptcy eliminates medical debt in Delaware. Chapter 7 vs Chapter 13, medical bills in collections, hospital liens, and your rights.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Medical Debt Is Fully Dischargeable

Medical debt is classified as general unsecured debt under bankruptcy law. It has no special protections and is fully eliminated in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy. In Delaware, as in every state, the federal Bankruptcy Code controls how medical debt is treated.

Chapter 7: Medical debt is wiped out in 3-4 months with no repayment

Chapter 13: Medical debt is included in your repayment plan; you may pay only a fraction of what you owe, and any remaining balance is discharged

No minimum amount: You can file regardless of how much medical debt you have

Medical Debt in Delaware: Key Facts

Before Filing

Medical bills can be sent to collections

Providers can sue and obtain judgments

Judgment liens may attach to your property

Wages can be garnished (15% of disposable earnings (more protective than federal 25%))

Credit score damage from unpaid bills

After Filing

Automatic stay stops all collection activity

Lawsuits are halted immediately

Garnishment stops

Judgment liens may be avoidable (section 522(f))

Discharge eliminates the debt permanently

Chapter 7 vs Chapter 13 for Medical Debt

Chapter 7 - Quick Elimination

If you qualify for Chapter 7 (pass the means test), medical debt is eliminated in 3-4 months with no repayment required. This is usually the fastest and simplest option for medical-debt-only situations.

Chapter 13 - Structured Repayment

If your income is above the means test threshold, or if you need to protect assets that exceed Delaware's exemptions, Chapter 13 lets you repay a portion of your medical debt over 3-5 years. Any remaining balance is discharged at plan completion.

Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy eliminate medical debt in Delaware?

Yes. Medical debt is unsecured debt and is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy. It is one of the most common reasons people file for bankruptcy nationwide.

Is medical debt treated differently in bankruptcy?

No. Medical debt is treated the same as credit card debt, personal loans, and other unsecured obligations. It receives no special priority and is eliminated in the discharge. There is no minimum or maximum amount of medical debt required to file.

Can I file bankruptcy just for medical bills?

Yes. You do not need to have multiple types of debt to file. Many people file bankruptcy solely because of medical bills. Medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy filings in the United States.

What happens to medical debt in collections?

Medical debt that has been sent to a collection agency is still dischargeable. Once you file, the automatic stay stops all collection activity. After discharge, the debt is eliminated and the collector cannot contact you again.

Can a hospital put a lien on my house for medical bills?

In Delaware, hospitals and medical providers can sometimes obtain a judgment lien on your property if they sue you and win. However, such liens may be avoidable in bankruptcy if they impair your homestead exemption under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f).

Will bankruptcy stop medical debt lawsuits?

Yes. Filing bankruptcy immediately triggers the automatic stay, which halts all pending lawsuits including medical debt collection cases. If a judgment has already been entered, the underlying debt is still dischargeable.

## Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/payday-loans.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Payday Loans

Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Delaware

Payday loans trap borrowers in cycles of high-interest debt. Bankruptcy can break that cycle by discharging payday loan obligations entirely.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Payday Loans Are Dischargeable

Payday loans are unsecured consumer debt. They are fully dischargeable in bankruptcy - meaning you will not owe anything after your case is completed.

Chapter 7: Payday loan debt is eliminated entirely in 3-4 months

Chapter 13: Payday loans are included in your repayment plan as general unsecured debt, typically receiving only a fraction of the balance

Stopping ACH Debits and Post-Dated Checks

Many payday lenders require ACH (electronic) authorization or post-dated checks as a condition of the loan. Once you file bankruptcy:

The automatic stay prohibits the lender from processing any further debits

Revoke your ACH authorization in writing with both the lender and your bank

Notify your bank of the bankruptcy filing and request a stop payment on any post-dated checks

If the lender processes a payment after filing, they have violated the stay and may owe you damages

Take action immediately upon filing: contact your bank, revoke ACH authorizations in writing, and keep copies of all communications.

Can Payday Lenders Challenge Discharge?

In rare cases, a payday lender might argue that the loan was obtained through fraud (11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)). To succeed, they would need to prove:

You made a false representation (e.g., lied about income or employment)

You intended to deceive the lender

The lender relied on your misrepresentation

In practice, payday lenders rarely challenge discharge because the amounts are small and the legal costs are high. Taking out a payday loan when you know you plan to file bankruptcy could raise issues, so consult an attorney about timing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge payday loans in bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes. Payday loans are unsecured debt and are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Delaware. Once discharged, the lender cannot collect, even if they have post-dated checks or ACH authorization.

Can a payday lender cash my check after I file bankruptcy?

No. The automatic stay prevents payday lenders from cashing post-dated checks or processing ACH debits after you file. If they do, it is a stay violation and you may be entitled to damages.

Should I stop paying payday loans before filing?

Once you decide to file bankruptcy, continuing to pay payday loans means less money available for necessary living expenses. However, consult an attorney about timing, especially regarding any pending ACH authorizations or post-dated checks.

Break the Payday Loan Cycle

Check if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge to eliminate payday loan debt.

Discharge Screener
