## Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Delaware - 60-Day Rescission, Options
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/reaffirmation-regret.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Delaware

Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Delaware

Reaffirmation agreements make you personally liable for a debt that would otherwise be discharged. If you regret signing one, you may have options - especially if you are still within the 60-day rescission window.

Quick answer: You can rescind (cancel) a reaffirmation agreement within 60 days of filing it with the court, or before discharge, whichever is later (11 U.S.C. section 524(c)(4)). No reason needed - just send written notice to the creditor. After 60 days, options are limited but include negotiation, voluntary surrender, or a new filing.

The 60-Day Rescission Window

Under 11 U.S.C. section 524(c)(4), you have an absolute right to rescind any reaffirmation agreement at any time before discharge or within 60 days after the agreement is filed with the court, whichever is later.

No reason required - you do not have to explain why

Written notice to the creditor is sufficient

The debt becomes dischargeable again once rescinded

The creditor may repossess the collateral (vehicle, etc.) after rescission

If you are considering rescission, act quickly. Count the days carefully from when the agreement was filed with the court (check your case docket), not when you signed it.

Options After the 60-Day Window

If You Cannot Afford the Payments

After the rescission window closes, you have several options:

Negotiate with the creditor: Request lower payments, reduced interest, or loan modification. Creditors often prefer this to repossession/foreclosure.

Refinance: If your credit has improved since filing, refinancing at a lower rate may make payments affordable.

Voluntary surrender: Return the property. You will owe any deficiency balance (the difference between what you owe and what the property sells for). This is a regular debt - not discharged because you reaffirmed it.

File a new bankruptcy: If eligible under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a)(8) (Chapter 7: 8 years between filings) or section 1328(f) (Chapter 13: varies), the reaffirmed debt can be discharged in the new case.

Delaware-Specific Considerations

Delaware allows federal exemptions, which affects the reaffirmation calculus. The federal motor vehicle exemption ($4,450) or Delaware's state motor vehicle exemption (Del. Code tit. 10, section 4902(b)(3), $15,000) should be compared. The federal wildcard ($1,475 plus up to $13,950 of unused homestead) can also protect vehicle equity. The 60-day rescission period applies in the District of Delaware.

Common Reaffirmation Regrets

Vehicle Reaffirmation

The most common reaffirmation regret involves vehicles. Debtors reaffirm a car loan to keep the vehicle but later realize the payment is unsustainable or the car is worth far less than the loan balance. Without reaffirmation, the debt would have been discharged - you could have surrendered the car and owed nothing.

Mortgage Reaffirmation

Most attorneys advise against mortgage reaffirmation. Without reaffirmation, you can keep making payments and stay in the home. If you ever need to walk away, the deficiency is discharged. With reaffirmation, you are personally liable for any deficiency after foreclosure.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I undo a reaffirmation agreement?

Within 60 days of filing or before discharge: yes, automatically. After that: options are limited to negotiation, refinancing, surrender, or a new bankruptcy filing.

What happens after the 60-day window?

The agreement is binding. You can negotiate with the creditor, refinance, voluntarily surrender, or file a new bankruptcy if eligible.

Do I need a reason to rescind?

No. The right to rescind within 60 days is absolute - no reason or justification needed. Simply send written notice to the creditor.

What if the court approved my reaffirmation?

Court approval does not prevent rescission during the 60-day window. The right to rescind under section 524(c)(4) applies regardless of court approval.

Should I have reaffirmed in the first place?

That depends on the specific asset and your financial situation. Many attorneys advise against reaffirmation (especially on mortgages) because it restores personal liability on debt that could have been discharged.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House

## Renting After Bankruptcy in Delaware - Landlord Screening, Tips, Tenant Rights
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/renting-after-bankruptcy.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Renting After Bankruptcy in Delaware

Renting After Bankruptcy in Delaware

Bankruptcy does not prevent you from renting. Many landlords will approve applicants with a bankruptcy on their record if you can show stable income and a reliable rental history. Here is how to navigate the process in Delaware.

Quick answer: You can rent after bankruptcy. Landlords can see the filing on your credit report, but it is not an automatic disqualifier. Focus on stable income, rental references, an honest explanation, and (if needed) a larger security deposit. Government housing assistance cannot be denied based on bankruptcy under section 525(a).

What Landlords See on Your Credit Report

When a landlord runs a credit check, they will see:

The bankruptcy filing - Chapter 7 (10 years) or Chapter 13 (7 years)

Discharged debts - accounts included in the bankruptcy will show as "included in bankruptcy" or "discharged"

Your credit score - typically drops significantly at filing but recovers over time

Post-filing payment history - new accounts opened after filing show your current reliability

Many landlords focus more on recent history than old entries. A bankruptcy from 2-3 years ago with perfect payment history since then is viewed very differently than a recent filing.

Delaware Tenant Rights and Protections

Delaware has the Delaware Residential Landlord-Tenant Code (Del. Code tit. 25, Ch. 55). Landlords can check credit reports including bankruptcy history. Delaware does not have statewide rent control. The Delaware Human Relations Act prohibits certain types of housing discrimination but does not list bankruptcy status. Many Delaware landlords, particularly in New Castle County, use credit screening services.

Tips for Getting Approved

1. Prepare a Rental Resume

Include your rental history, employment details, income verification, and references from previous landlords. A well-organized application signals reliability.

2. Write a Brief Explanation Letter

Address the bankruptcy directly. Explain the circumstances (job loss, medical emergency, divorce) and emphasize what has changed. Show that you have stable income and have been managing finances responsibly since filing.

3. Offer a Larger Security Deposit

Offering an additional month's deposit reduces the landlord's risk and shows good faith. Check Delaware law for any limits on security deposit amounts.

4. Consider Individual Landlords Over Large Complexes

Individual landlords and small property owners often have more flexibility in their screening criteria than large corporate-managed apartment complexes that use automated approval algorithms.

5. Get References

A letter from a previous landlord confirming on-time payments and good tenancy can outweigh a credit blemish. Employment references showing stable income also help.

Government Housing After Bankruptcy

Under 11 U.S.C. section 525(a), government agencies cannot deny public benefits based solely on a bankruptcy filing. This includes:

Section 8 Housing Choice Vouchers

Public housing

State housing assistance programs

FHA-insured mortgages (after applicable waiting periods)

The waiting period for FHA loans is 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge or 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan with court approval.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I rent after bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes. Many landlords will approve applicants with bankruptcy if you show stable income, good rental history, and an honest explanation of your filing.

How long does bankruptcy show on my credit report?

Chapter 7: 10 years. Chapter 13: 7 years. The impact on your score diminishes over time, especially with good post-filing payment history.

Can a landlord deny me for bankruptcy?

For new applications, landlords can consider bankruptcy as part of screening in most states. For renewals, some courts extend section 525 protections. Government housing cannot be denied based on bankruptcy.

Should I explain the bankruptcy to a landlord?

Yes. A brief, honest explanation shows responsibility and maturity. Emphasize what changed and your current financial stability.

Can I get Section 8 after bankruptcy?

Yes. Under section 525(a), government agencies cannot deny public benefits based on bankruptcy. Section 8, public housing, and other government housing programs remain available.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/repo-credit-report.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Repo & Credit Report

Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Delaware

Bankruptcy does not erase the repo from your credit report - but it eliminates the debt. Here is what changes, what stays, and how to fix errors.

Quick answer: The repossession entry stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. Bankruptcy discharges the remaining balance (deficiency), which should be reported as $0. If the creditor still reports a balance after discharge, dispute it under the FCRA.

What Changes After Discharge

What Bankruptcy Fixes

Deficiency balance discharged - you owe $0

Collection activity must stop permanently

Account status should update to "discharged"

No more calls, letters, or lawsuits about the balance

Creditor cannot report a balance owed

What Bankruptcy Does Not Fix

Repossession entry remains on credit report

Late payment history leading up to repo remains

7-year reporting clock does not reset

Bankruptcy filing itself appears on credit report

Credit score impact from both events

Disputing Credit Report Errors

After bankruptcy discharge, review your credit reports from all three bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) for these common errors:

Balance still showing: Should be $0 after discharge. This is the most common error.

"Past due" status: Should show "discharged in bankruptcy" or "included in bankruptcy."

Active collection: The account should not show as in active collection after discharge.

Incorrect dates: The date of first delinquency determines when the entry falls off. Verify this date is correct.

1

Pull Reports

Get free reports from annualcreditreport.com (all three bureaus)

2

Identify Errors

Compare each repo account against your discharge order

3

Dispute

File disputes online with each bureau. Include your discharge order as evidence.

FCRA Rights After Bankruptcy

The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) gives you powerful tools to correct your credit report after bankruptcy:

Dispute right: You can dispute any inaccurate information. The bureau has 30 days to investigate and correct or remove the entry.

Creditor obligations: Creditors who report to credit bureaus must update accounts to reflect the discharge. Failure to do so violates the FCRA.

Damages: If a creditor willfully reports incorrect information after discharge, you may be entitled to statutory damages ($100-$1,000), actual damages, and attorney fees under the FCRA.

Discharge injunction: Reporting a balance owed on a discharged debt may also violate the bankruptcy discharge injunction (11 U.S.C. section 524), which can result in contempt sanctions.

Rebuilding Credit After Repo + Bankruptcy

Secured credit card: Start with a secured card ($200-$500 deposit). Use it for small purchases and pay in full monthly.

Credit-builder loan: Some Delaware credit unions offer loans specifically designed to rebuild credit.

Authorized user: Ask a trusted person to add you as an authorized user on their account.

On-time payments: Every on-time payment builds your score. Payment history is the largest factor (35%).

Time: The repo and bankruptcy impact your score less as they age. Most people see significant score improvement within 2-3 years of discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does bankruptcy remove a repossession from my credit report in Delaware?

No. Bankruptcy does not remove the repossession entry from your credit report. The repo stays on your report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. However, the bankruptcy discharge eliminates the deficiency balance - the amount you still owe after the repo sale.

What happens to the repo balance after bankruptcy?

In Chapter 7, the deficiency balance (what you owe minus what the vehicle sold for) is discharged. In Chapter 13, it is treated as unsecured debt and paid at the plan percentage, often cents on the dollar. Either way, the balance should be reported as $0 after discharge.

How should a repo appear on my credit report after discharge?

After bankruptcy discharge, the repossession account should show: balance $0, 'included in bankruptcy' or 'discharged in bankruptcy.' If the report still shows a balance owed, that is a reporting error you can dispute.

Can I dispute a repo on my credit report after bankruptcy?

Yes. Under the FCRA (Fair Credit Reporting Act), you can dispute inaccurate information. If the repo account shows a balance after discharge, shows as 'past due' rather than 'discharged,' or has incorrect dates, file disputes with all three bureaus.

How long does a repo stay on my credit report in Delaware?

A repossession stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency, regardless of bankruptcy. The bankruptcy filing itself stays for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7). These are federal rules that apply uniformly across all states including Delaware.

Can the repo lender collect after bankruptcy discharge?

No. The discharge injunction under 11 U.S.C. section 524 permanently prohibits the creditor from collecting the discharged debt. If they attempt collection (calls, letters, reporting a balance), you may have a claim for violation of the discharge injunction.

Related Topics

## Vehicle Repossession and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/repossession.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Repossession

Stop Repossession in Delaware with Bankruptcy

How bankruptcy stops vehicle repossession in Delaware. Get your car back, Chapter 13 cramdown, reaffirmation, and your rights under Delaware law.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Stopping Repossession in Delaware

The moment you file bankruptcy in the District of Delaware, the automatic stay takes effect and all repossession activity must stop. If a repo agent is literally on the way to take your car, filing stops them in their tracks.

Delaware vehicle exemption: $15,000 in equity per debtor

Before vs. After Repossession

Car Not Yet Repossessed

Automatic stay prevents repossession

File before the repo happens

Continue making payments (reaffirm) or use Chapter 13 to catch up

Vehicle stays in your possession

Car Already Repossessed

File immediately - before the car is sold

Court may order turnover under section 542

Lender must stop the sale process

Chapter 13 can structure catch-up payments

If already sold, deficiency is dischargeable

Your Options by Chapter

Chapter 7 Options

Reaffirmation: Keep paying the loan as before. Car is yours but debt survives.

Redemption: Pay the car's current fair market value in a lump sum.

Surrender: Give back the car and discharge the remaining balance.

Chapter 13 Options

Catch up on payments: Spread missed payments over 3-5 year plan.

Cramdown: Reduce loan balance to current value (if purchased 910+ days ago).

Lower interest rate: Court may approve a lower rate than your original loan.

Surrender: Give back the car and treat deficiency as unsecured debt.

Keep Your Car Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop repossession in Delaware?

Yes. Filing bankruptcy immediately triggers the automatic stay, which stops all repossession attempts. If you file before the vehicle is repossessed, the lender cannot take it. Delaware's vehicle exemption protects up to $15,000 in equity.

Can I get my car back after repossession in Delaware?

If your car has been repossessed but not yet sold, filing bankruptcy may force the lender to return it. The automatic stay requires the lender to stop all collection activity, and courts in the District of Delaware may order turnover of the vehicle under 11 U.S.C. section 542.

How does Chapter 13 help with repossession?

Chapter 13 lets you keep your vehicle and catch up on missed payments through your 3-5 year plan. If the vehicle was purchased more than 910 days ago, you can cramdown the loan to the car's current value, potentially saving thousands.

What is the 910-day rule for car loans?

If you purchased your car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing Chapter 13, you can reduce the loan balance to the vehicle's fair market value. If purchased within 910 days, you must pay the full loan balance.

What happens if my car is repossessed and sold?

If the car has already been sold, you may owe a deficiency balance (the difference between what the car sold for and what you owed). This deficiency is unsecured debt and can be discharged in bankruptcy.

What is Delaware's vehicle exemption?

Delaware's vehicle exemption protects up to $15,000 in equity. If your equity is within this amount, the bankruptcy trustee cannot sell your vehicle in Chapter 7.

Browse Other States

Alaska
Arizona
Arkansas
California
Colorado

View All States →

## Delaware Bankruptcy Resources - Courts, Legal Aid, Credit Counseling
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/resources.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Resources

Delaware Bankruptcy Resources

Courts, legal aid organizations, credit counseling agencies, and free educational tools to help you navigate the bankruptcy process in Delaware.

Bankruptcy Courts

District of Delaware

Address: 824 N. Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801

Phone: (302) 252-2900

Website: deb.uscourts.gov

Legal Aid Organizations

Delaware Volunteer Legal Services

Pro bono legal assistance for low-income Delaware residents.

Phone: (302) 478-8680

Website: dvls.org

Legal Services Corporation of Delaware

Free civil legal help for qualifying residents.

Phone: (302) 575-0408

Website: lscd.com

Credit Counseling and Debtor Education

Two courses are required for bankruptcy: a pre-filing credit counseling course and a post-filing debtor education course. You must use agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee Program.

Approved agencies: justice.gov - approved agencies

Cost: $15-25 per course (reduced fees available)

Format: Online, phone, or in-person

Time: About 60-90 minutes per course

U.S. Trustee Program

The U.S. Trustee Program oversees the administration of bankruptcy cases.

Region 3: (302) 573-6491

Website: justice.gov/ust

Free Educational Tools

Automatic Stay Guide

Means Test Explained

National Cost Guide

How to File Bankruptcy

Case Dismissed?

File Again?

Non-Dischargeable Debts

Dismissal Rate Data

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the bankruptcy court in Delaware?

Delaware has 1 federal bankruptcy court. D. Del.: 824 N. Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801 ((302) 252-2900)

Is there free legal help for bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes. Delaware Volunteer Legal Services ((302) 478-8680) provides free legal services. Legal Services Corporation of Delaware ((302) 575-0408) provides free legal services.

Where can I take the required credit counseling course?

The U.S. Trustee Program maintains a list of approved credit counseling agencies at justice.gov/ust. Most offer courses online or by phone for $15-25.

## Can I Keep My 401k and Retirement in Delaware Bankruptcy?
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/retirement-accounts.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Retirement Accounts and Bankruptcy in Delaware

Retirement Accounts and Bankruptcy in Delaware

Your retirement savings are among the most protected assets in bankruptcy. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law, and IRAs are protected up to $1,512,350. Here is what Delaware filers need to know.

Quick answer: Your 401(k), 403(b), pension, and other ERISA-qualified retirement plans are 100% exempt in bankruptcy - no dollar limit. IRAs (traditional and Roth) are protected up to $1,512,350. Do NOT cash out retirement to pay debts or avoid filing. That converts protected money into unprotected money.

ERISA Plans: 100% Protected

Under federal law (11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3)(C) and ERISA section 206(d)), employer-sponsored retirement plans are completely exempt from the bankruptcy estate. This includes:

401(k) plans - no dollar limit on exemption

403(b) plans (tax-sheltered annuities) - fully exempt

Defined benefit pension plans - fully exempt

Profit-sharing plans - fully exempt

457(b) government plans - fully exempt

Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) - fully exempt for federal employees

This protection applies nationwide regardless of state exemption choices. The trustee cannot touch these accounts.

IRAs: Protected Up to $1,512,350

Traditional IRAs and Roth IRAs are protected under 11 U.S.C. section 522(n) up to a combined limit of $1,512,350 (adjusted for inflation). This limit applies to:

Traditional IRAs

Roth IRAs

SEP-IRAs (may receive full ERISA protection if employer-sponsored)

SIMPLE IRAs (may receive full ERISA protection)

Rollover amounts from an ERISA plan into an IRA retain their unlimited ERISA protection - they do not count against the $1,512,350 cap.

Delaware-Specific Retirement Protections

Delaware allows filers to choose federal exemptions, making the federal IRA exemption ($1,512,350) available. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under both state and federal law. Delaware state employee pensions (under Del. Code tit. 29, Ch. 55) are fully exempt. The choice between state and federal exemptions can affect retirement protection - consult the full exemption picture before choosing.

Important: Delaware uses state or federal exemptions. If you have a choice between state and federal exemptions, compare the total exemption picture - retirement is just one factor.

The Biggest Mistake: Cashing Out Retirement

Every year, thousands of people cash out retirement accounts to pay debts or avoid bankruptcy. This is almost always a mistake because:

You lose the exemption. Money in a retirement account is protected. Money in a bank account is not (or minimally protected).

You owe income taxes on the entire withdrawal amount.

You pay a 10% early withdrawal penalty if under age 59 1/2.

The debts may be dischargeable anyway. Credit card debt, medical debt, and personal loans are wiped out in bankruptcy - you could have kept the retirement money and discharged the debt.

You cannot replace the money. Annual contribution limits mean it takes years to rebuild what you withdrew.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my 401(k) in Delaware bankruptcy?

Yes. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law. Your 401(k) is completely protected regardless of balance.

Are IRAs protected?

Yes, up to $1,512,350 combined for all traditional and Roth IRAs. Rollover amounts from ERISA plans keep their unlimited protection.

Can I contribute to retirement during Chapter 13?

Generally yes at your existing level. Increasing contributions during the plan may be challenged by the trustee as reducing disposable income.

What about inherited IRAs?

Inherited IRAs are NOT protected in bankruptcy (Clark v. Rameker, 2014). Only IRAs you funded yourself are exempt. Spousal inherited IRAs may be protected if rolled into your own IRA.

Should I cash out retirement to avoid bankruptcy?

Almost never. You lose the exemption, owe taxes, pay penalties, and the debt might have been dischargeable. Talk to a bankruptcy attorney before touching retirement accounts.

Can creditors garnish retirement accounts?

ERISA plans are generally protected from garnishment. Exceptions: IRS tax levies, divorce QDROs, and certain federal criminal penalties.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/second-mortgage.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Second Mortgage

Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in Delaware

If your Delaware home is worth less than your first mortgage, Chapter 13 lien stripping can eliminate your second mortgage entirely.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is Lien Stripping?

Lien stripping is a powerful tool available in Chapter 13 bankruptcy under 11 U.S.C. section 506(a). It allows you to remove a junior mortgage (second mortgage, HELOC, or other junior lien) from your home when:

Your home's current fair market value is less than the balance on your first mortgage

The second mortgage is therefore wholly unsecured - there is no equity supporting it

You file a motion with the D. Del. court asking to strip the lien

Once the Chapter 13 plan is completed, the second mortgage lien is permanently removed from your property.

How It Works

1

Get Home Appraised

Determine your home's fair market value. It must be less than the first mortgage balance for the second mortgage to qualify for stripping.

2

File Lien Strip Motion

Your attorney files a motion to value the collateral and strip the wholly unsecured junior lien. The second mortgage holder can object.

3

Complete the Plan

The second mortgage is treated as unsecured debt in your 3-5 year plan. Upon plan completion and discharge, the lien is permanently removed.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Second Mortgages

Chapter 7

Lien stripping is not available in Chapter 7 per Bank of America v. Caulkett (2015). The personal liability on the second mortgage can be discharged, but the lien survives. The creditor cannot collect from you personally, but the lien remains attached to the property.

Chapter 13

Lien stripping is available. If the home value is below the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage lien is stripped and the debt is treated as unsecured. Upon successful plan completion, the lien is permanently removed from your title.

Delaware Homestead Exemption Impact

The Delaware homestead exemption of $125,000 protects your home equity from creditors. In a lien stripping scenario, the exemption is less relevant because the home is underwater - but it becomes critical if property values recover.

Learn more about protecting your home in Delaware →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I strip a second mortgage in bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes, through a process called lien stripping in Chapter 13. If your home is worth less than what you owe on your first mortgage, the second mortgage is considered wholly unsecured and can be treated as general unsecured debt in your plan. At completion of the plan, the lien is removed.

Can I strip a second mortgage in Chapter 7?

No. The Supreme Court ruled in Bank of America v. Caulkett (2015) that Chapter 7 debtors cannot strip wholly underwater junior liens. Lien stripping is only available in Chapter 13 (and Chapter 11) in Delaware.

What if I have some equity above the first mortgage?

If your home value exceeds the first mortgage balance even slightly, the second mortgage is not wholly unsecured and cannot be stripped. The full second mortgage balance would need to be addressed in your Chapter 13 plan or reaffirmed.

Explore Lien Stripping Options

Learn more about how lien stripping can save your home and reduce your debt.

Lien Stripping Guide
Delaware Chapter 13 Guide

## Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Delaware - Will I Lose My Clearance?
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/security-clearance.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Delaware

Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Delaware

Filing bankruptcy does not automatically revoke your security clearance. In many cases, resolving debt through bankruptcy can actually help your clearance by eliminating financial vulnerability. Here is what clearance holders in Delaware need to know.

Quick answer: Bankruptcy does NOT automatically revoke your security clearance. The adjudicative guidelines recognize that taking responsible steps to resolve debt - including filing bankruptcy - can be a mitigating factor. Unresolved, spiraling debt is a bigger clearance risk than filing bankruptcy to resolve it.

Guideline F: Financial Considerations

Security clearance adjudications are governed by the Adjudicative Guidelines, specifically Guideline F (Financial Considerations). The concern is that financial problems can indicate poor self-control, unreliability, or vulnerability to pressure.

However, Guideline F also lists mitigating conditions, including:

The behavior happened under unusual circumstances (job loss, medical emergency, divorce) and is unlikely to recur

The individual has initiated and is adhering to a good-faith effort to resolve debts - bankruptcy qualifies

The conditions that resulted in the financial problem were largely beyond the person's control

There are clear indications the problem is being resolved or is under control

Filing bankruptcy directly addresses multiple mitigating conditions. It eliminates the debt, demonstrates proactive behavior, and removes the financial vulnerability that is the actual clearance concern.

Delaware Military and Defense Presence

Delaware hosts Dover Air Force Base, a major military installation. Various defense contractors operate in the state. The DuPont and chemical industry corridor also has positions requiring security clearance. Filing bankruptcy does not automatically revoke clearance and can demonstrate financial responsibility.

Self-Reporting Requirements

If you currently hold a security clearance, you are typically required to self-report significant financial events. This includes:

Filing for bankruptcy (either chapter)

Judgments or liens against you

Significant delinquencies (typically 120+ days past due)

Wage garnishments

Report through your Facility Security Officer (FSO) or Security Manager. Timely self-reporting demonstrates integrity and compliance - two qualities adjudicators value highly.

Failing to report a bankruptcy filing when required is a separate security concern. The cover-up is worse than the event itself. Always report promptly.

Why Bankruptcy Can Help Your Clearance

Without Bankruptcy

Growing debt creates ongoing vulnerability

Debt collectors and creditors pursuing you

Potential garnishments and judgments

Financial stress affects job performance

Adjudicator sees unresolved problems

After Bankruptcy

Debt eliminated or structured into a plan

No more creditor pressure or coercion risk

Clean financial slate going forward

Demonstrates proactive problem-solving

Adjudicator sees responsible resolution

Frequently Asked Questions

Will I lose my security clearance if I file bankruptcy?

No. Bankruptcy does not automatically revoke clearance. It can demonstrate financial responsibility by resolving debt that creates vulnerability.

Should I tell my security officer?

Yes. Self-reporting is typically required and demonstrates integrity. Failure to report is a separate concern.

Can bankruptcy help my clearance?

Yes. Eliminating debt removes financial vulnerability - the actual clearance concern. Adjudicators recognize bankruptcy as a responsible step.

Is Chapter 13 better than Chapter 7 for clearance?

Chapter 13 is sometimes viewed more favorably because it shows a repayment commitment. But Chapter 7 is also recognized as a legitimate debt resolution method.

Can I get a new clearance after bankruptcy?

Yes. Many people receive clearances after bankruptcy. Adjudicators evaluate the whole person, not just the bankruptcy filing.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Small Business Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/small-business.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Small Business

Small Business Bankruptcy in Delaware

Guide to small business bankruptcy in Delaware. Chapter 7 liquidation, Chapter 11 Subchapter V, Chapter 13 for sole proprietors, and protecting your business.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Small Business Bankruptcy Options in Delaware

Chapter 7 - Liquidation

Business closes and assets are sold

Fastest resolution (3-4 months)

Sole proprietors receive personal discharge

LLCs/corps do NOT receive a discharge

Filing fee: $338

Subchapter V (Ch. 11)

Streamlined reorganization

Debts under $7,500,000

Owner retains equity

No creditor committee

3-5 year repayment plan

Filing fee: $1,738

Traditional Chapter 11

Full reorganization for larger businesses

More expensive and complex

Creditor committee involvement

Greater court oversight

Filing fee: $1,738

Chapter 13 (Sole Proprietors)

Available only to sole proprietors

Debt limits apply (currently ~$2.75M)

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep business assets

Filing fee: $313

Subchapter V - The Game Changer

The Small Business Reorganization Act of 2019 created Subchapter V of Chapter 11, specifically designed for small businesses. It has fundamentally changed small business bankruptcy by making reorganization accessible and affordable.

Key Advantages

Speed: Plan must be filed within 90 days (vs. 18+ months in traditional Chapter 11)

Cost: No creditor committee, fewer contested matters, lower attorney fees

Owner retains equity: The absolute priority rule does not apply - owners can keep their interest without paying unsecured creditors in full

Debtor-in-possession: You continue running your business during the case

Consensual or cramdown: Plan can be confirmed even without creditor consent if it commits all disposable income for 3-5 years

Filing in the District of Delaware

Courthouse: 824 Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801

Small business cases in the District of Delaware are assigned a Subchapter V trustee who facilitates the reorganization process. The trustee's role is to help the debtor and creditors reach a consensual plan, not to liquidate assets.

Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

What are my options for small business bankruptcy in Delaware?

Small businesses in Delaware can file Chapter 7 (liquidation), Chapter 11 (reorganization), Chapter 11 Subchapter V (streamlined for small businesses with debts under $7,500,000), or Chapter 13 (for sole proprietors with debts under the limit). The best choice depends on whether you want to continue or close the business.

What is Subchapter V bankruptcy?

Subchapter V of Chapter 11 (created by the Small Business Reorganization Act of 2019) is a streamlined reorganization for businesses with debts under $7,500,000. It is faster, cheaper, and more debtor-friendly than traditional Chapter 11. There is no committee of unsecured creditors, and the owner can retain equity without paying creditors in full.

Can I keep my business if I file bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes, if you file Chapter 11 or Subchapter V. These chapters allow you to reorganize debt while continuing to operate. Chapter 13 also works for sole proprietors. In Chapter 7, the business typically closes unless it has no non-exempt assets.

What happens to business debts if I am a sole proprietor?

Sole proprietors are personally liable for all business debts. Filing personal bankruptcy discharges both personal and business debts. If your business is a corporation or LLC, only the entity's debts are addressed in the entity's bankruptcy - your personal liability depends on whether you personally guaranteed the debts.

Can I file bankruptcy for my LLC?

Yes, LLCs can file Chapter 7 (liquidation) or Chapter 11/Subchapter V (reorganization) in the District of Delaware. However, LLCs do not receive a discharge in Chapter 7 - the entity is simply liquidated. Subchapter V is typically the best option for an LLC that wants to continue operating.

How much does small business bankruptcy cost?

Chapter 7 business filing fee is $338. Chapter 11 Subchapter V filing fee is $1,738. Attorney fees in Delaware for Subchapter V typically range from $10,000 to $30,000 depending on complexity. Chapter 7 business attorney fees typically range from $2,000 to $5,000.

Browse Other States

Alabama

## Delaware Bankruptcy Statistics - Filing Data and Trends
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/statistics.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Statistics

Delaware Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008-2024). Delaware's consumer bankruptcy filing rate is moderate. The district is more widely known for its large corporate Chapter 11 cases.

1

Federal District

$125,000

Homestead Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Delaware Federal Court District

DistrictCases (2008-2024)Primary Location

District of Delaware (D. Del.)over 15,000Wilmington

Key Facts About Bankruptcy in Delaware

Homestead exemption: $125,000 - Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 of equity in your primary residence.

Chapter 13 dismissal risk: Nationally, about one-third to two-fifths of Chapter 13 cases end in dismissal. Quality of legal representation is one of the strongest predictors of plan completion.

Prior filer rate: Across the nation, roughly 25-30% of Chapter 13 filers had a prior bankruptcy case, suggesting many debtors cycle through the system without achieving lasting relief.

Means test: Delaware's median income for a single filer is approximately $55,000/year.

Understanding the Data

Chapter 13 plans run 3-5 years. Cases filed in recent years (2020-2024) are still active if succeeding - only early dismissals have been counted. The most reliable dismissal rates come from mature years (2008-2019) where most cases have reached final outcomes.

Source: Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database, 2008-2024. Analysis by Open Bankruptcy Project.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many bankruptcy cases are filed in Delaware?

Since 2008, Delaware's district has processed over 15,000+ bankruptcy cases. Filing volumes peaked around 2010-2011 following the financial crisis and have generally declined since.

What is the Chapter 13 success rate?

Nationally, about 33-40% of Chapter 13 cases are dismissed before completion. Rates vary by district and depend on factors including the local legal market, judicial practices, and the complexity of cases filed. National dismissal rate data.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## What Bills to Stop Paying Before Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/stop-paying-bills.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Stop Paying Bills

What Bills to Stop Paying Before Bankruptcy in Delaware

Not all debts are created equal. Knowing which bills to stop paying - and which to keep paying - can save you thousands before you file.

Quick answer: Stop paying unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) that will be discharged. Keep paying secured debts you want to keep (mortgage, car loan) and essential services (utilities, insurance). Do not make large payments to any single creditor in the 90 days before filing.

Bills You Can Stop Paying

Credit cards: All credit card debt is dischargeable. Stop paying, but stop using the cards too. New charges near filing can be challenged as fraud.

Medical bills: Medical debt is unsecured and fully dischargeable. Collections calls are annoying but temporary.

Personal loans: Unsecured personal loans from banks, online lenders, and finance companies are dischargeable.

Old utility bills: Past-due amounts on closed accounts are dischargeable. (Keep current utilities paid - see below.)

Payday loans: Dischargeable unsecured debt. Payday lenders may threaten criminal action but cannot follow through on threats related to civil debt.

Collection accounts: Debts already in collections are dischargeable. The collection agency loses its right to collect once you file.

Bills You Should Keep Paying

Mortgage (if keeping the house): Falling behind creates arrears that complicate your case. In Chapter 13, you must be current on post-petition payments.

Car loan (if keeping the car): Same logic. Stay current on vehicles you need for work and daily life.

Current utilities: Electricity, water, gas, internet. Utility companies can require a deposit after bankruptcy but cannot shut off service solely because you filed.

Insurance: Auto insurance is legally required in Delaware. Health insurance protects against new medical debt.

Child support/alimony: These are not dischargeable and must be paid. Falling behind creates a priority debt that must be paid in full in Chapter 13.

Taxes: Recent income taxes (within 3 years) are priority debts. Older tax debts may be dischargeable under specific conditions.

The 90-Day Preference Period

Under 11 U.S.C. section 547, the bankruptcy trustee can claw back payments made to creditors during the 90 days before filing if they exceed $600 to a single creditor. For payments to insiders (family, business partners), the look-back period is one year.

Common mistake: Paying off a credit card or repaying a family loan right before filing. The trustee can sue the recipient to recover that money for the benefit of all creditors. This is called a preferential transfer.

$600+ to one creditor in 90 days - potentially recoverable

Any amount to family in 12 months - potentially recoverable

Regular, ordinary payments (mortgage, car loan, utilities) - generally safe

Frequently Asked Questions

What bills should I stop paying before bankruptcy in Delaware?

Generally, you should continue paying secured debts you want to keep (mortgage, car loan) and essential bills (utilities, insurance). You can typically stop paying unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) that will be discharged in bankruptcy. Consult with a bankruptcy attorney before making changes.

Should I stop paying credit cards before filing bankruptcy?

Yes, in most cases. Credit card debt is unsecured and will be discharged in bankruptcy. Continuing to pay credit cards before filing wastes money that could go toward filing costs or essential expenses. However, stop making new charges on the cards - using credit cards with no intent to repay is fraud.

What is the preference period in bankruptcy?

The preference period is 90 days before filing (1 year for payments to insiders like family members). Payments of $600 or more to a single creditor during this period can be clawed back by the trustee as preferential transfers. Paying a favorite creditor right before filing can backfire.

Should I keep paying my mortgage before bankruptcy?

If you want to keep your house, yes - keep paying the mortgage. If you plan to surrender the house, you can stop paying. In Chapter 13, mortgage arrears can be cured through the plan, but you must stay current on post-petition payments.

Should I keep paying my car loan before bankruptcy?

If you want to keep the vehicle, continue making payments. If you plan to surrender it, you can stop. In Chapter 13, you can cure arrears through the plan and may be able to reduce the loan balance to the vehicle's value (cramdown) if the loan is more than 910 days old.

Can creditors sue me if I stop paying before filing?

Yes, creditors can file lawsuits and obtain judgments. However, once you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay halts all collection activity. If you plan to file within 30-60 days, the risk of a completed lawsuit is low. A judgment lien, however, can complicate your bankruptcy.

Related Topics

Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House

Garnishment

## Student Loans and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/student-loans.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Student Loans

Student Loans and Bankruptcy in Delaware

Can you discharge student loans in bankruptcy in Delaware? The Brunner test, undue hardship, income-driven repayment, and recent developments.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Undue Hardship Standard

Student loans - both federal and private - can be discharged in bankruptcy if you prove undue hardship through an adversary proceeding (a separate lawsuit within your bankruptcy case). In the District of Delaware, the court applies the Brunner test.

The Three Brunner Prongs

Minimal standard of living: You cannot maintain a minimal standard of living for yourself and your dependents if forced to repay

Persistence: Your financial hardship is likely to continue for a significant portion of the repayment period

Good faith: You have made good-faith efforts to repay the loans

Recent Developments

DOJ Guidance (November 2022)

The Department of Justice issued new guidance creating a more standardized process for evaluating student loan discharge. Debtors can now submit an attestation form, and the DOJ has instructed its attorneys to consent to discharge when the evidence supports it. This represents a significant shift from the historical near-impossibility of student loan discharge.

Income-Driven Repayment as an Alternative

If discharge through bankruptcy is not viable, federal income-driven repayment (IDR) plans can reduce monthly payments to as low as $0 based on your income. After 20-25 years of payments, any remaining balance is forgiven. The SAVE plan (effective 2024) offers the most generous terms.

Student Loans by Chapter

Chapter 7

Must file separate adversary proceeding

Prove undue hardship (Brunner test)

If successful, loans are discharged

If unsuccessful, loans survive but other debts are eliminated

No repayment plan - faster resolution

Chapter 13

Can file adversary proceeding for discharge

Student loans can be included in repayment plan

May pay reduced amount during plan

Automatic stay stops collection during plan

Unpaid balance survives unless hardship proven

Student Loan Bankruptcy Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

Can student loans be discharged in bankruptcy in Delaware?

Student loans can be discharged in bankruptcy in Delaware, but only if you prove undue hardship to the court through an adversary proceeding. The standard used in the District of Delaware is the Brunner test, which requires showing you cannot maintain a minimal standard of living, your situation is likely to persist, and you have made good-faith efforts to repay.

What is the Brunner test?

The Brunner test requires proving three things: (1) you cannot maintain a minimal standard of living for yourself and your dependents if forced to repay, (2) your financial situation is likely to persist for a significant portion of the repayment period, and (3) you have made good-faith efforts to repay the loans.

What about the new DOJ guidance on student loans?

In November 2022, the DOJ issued new guidance making it easier to discharge student loans in bankruptcy. The DOJ now uses a standardized process to evaluate undue hardship claims, and debtors can submit an attestation form. This has made discharges more accessible than before.

Should I include student loans in my bankruptcy filing?

Yes. Even if you cannot discharge them, listing student loans in your bankruptcy stops collection activity during the case through the automatic stay. In Chapter 13, student loan payments can be structured into your repayment plan, sometimes at reduced amounts.

What happens to student loans in Chapter 13?

In Chapter 13, student loans are treated as general unsecured claims unless you file a separate adversary proceeding for discharge. You can propose paying a reduced amount through your plan, but any unpaid balance survives the bankruptcy unless you prove undue hardship.

Are private student loans treated differently?

Private student loans and federal student loans are both subject to the same undue hardship standard for discharge. However, some courts have been more willing to discharge private student loans, particularly if the school was not an eligible institution.

Browse Other States

## Surrendering Your Car in Delaware Bankruptcy - Process, Deficiency, Options
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/surrender-car.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Surrendering Your Car in Delaware Bankruptcy

Surrendering Your Car in Delaware Bankruptcy

Surrendering a vehicle in bankruptcy eliminates both the car payment and any deficiency balance. This can be the right choice when you owe more than the car is worth or cannot afford the payments.

Quick answer: Surrender means returning the vehicle to the lender. In Chapter 7, the deficiency balance (what you owe minus what the car sells for) is discharged - you owe nothing. In Chapter 13, the deficiency becomes unsecured debt in your plan. Surrender is often the smartest move for upside-down loans.

How Car Surrender Works

Step-by-Step Process

File bankruptcy and indicate "surrender" on your Statement of Intention (Chapter 7) or plan (Chapter 13)

Automatic stay activates - the lender cannot repossess while the stay is in effect

Return the vehicle - the lender arranges pickup or designates a drop-off location (usually 30-45 days after filing)

Lender sells the vehicle - typically at auction, often for wholesale value

Deficiency balance calculated - what you owe minus sale price

Deficiency discharged - in Chapter 7, the deficiency is eliminated; in Chapter 13, it becomes unsecured debt in your plan

Delaware Vehicle Exemptions

Delaware offers a choice between state exemptions (motor vehicle exemption: $15,000) and federal exemptions ($4,450 vehicle + wildcard). This choice is critical when deciding whether to keep or surrender a vehicle. The deficiency after surrender is discharged in Chapter 7. Delaware is a small state with public transit concentrated in Wilmington - rural areas require personal vehicles.

When Surrender Makes Sense

Consider Surrender When

You owe significantly more than the car is worth (upside down)

The payment is unaffordable

The vehicle has high mileage or needs expensive repairs

You have access to alternative transportation

You can acquire a cheaper replacement vehicle

Interest rate is very high (20%+ on a buy-here-pay-here loan)

Consider Keeping When

You have positive equity (worth more than you owe)

The payment is affordable on your budget

The vehicle is reliable and you need it for work

You are close to paying it off

Chapter 13 cramdown can reduce the balance to current value

Interest rate is reasonable

The Chapter 13 Cramdown Alternative

If your car loan is more than 910 days old (approximately 2.5 years), Chapter 13 offers a powerful alternative to surrender: the cramdown. Under 11 U.S.C. section 1325(a), you can reduce the secured claim to the vehicle's current value and pay that amount through your plan at a court-approved interest rate.

Example: You owe $15,000 on a car worth $8,000. If the loan is older than 910 days, you pay $8,000 as a secured claim in your plan. The remaining $7,000 becomes unsecured debt (paid at whatever percentage your plan pays unsecured creditors - often pennies on the dollar).

The 910-day rule (sometimes called the "hanging paragraph") prevents cramdown on newer vehicle purchases.

Getting a Replacement Vehicle

After surrender, you will need replacement transportation. Options include:

Buy-here-pay-here lots: Available immediately but often have high interest rates and limited selection

Cash purchase: Save up for a reliable used car ($3,000-$5,000 can get a dependable vehicle)

Credit union auto loans: Some credit unions will lend to recent bankruptcy filers, especially members

Subprime auto lenders: Available after discharge, interest rates will be high but can be refinanced later as credit improves

Family or friends: A temporary arrangement while you rebuild

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens when I surrender my car?

You return it, the lender sells it, and the deficiency is discharged in Chapter 7 or becomes unsecured debt in Chapter 13.

Do I have to surrender?

No. You can reaffirm, redeem, or (in Chapter 13) cramdown. Surrender is one option among several.

When do I give up the car?

Typically 30-45 days after filing. The lender arranges pickup or a drop-off. You can drive until then.

Will I owe after surrender?

In Chapter 7, no - the deficiency is discharged. In Chapter 13, it becomes unsecured debt in your plan.

Can I surrender a leased car?

Yes. You can reject the lease in bankruptcy. Early termination fees and remaining lease obligations are dischargeable.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Tax Debt and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/tax-debt.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Tax Debt

Tax Debt and Bankruptcy in Delaware

Can you discharge tax debt in Delaware bankruptcy? IRS rules, the 3-year/2-year/240-day tests, priority vs. non-priority taxes, and state tax obligations.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Which Tax Debts Can Be Discharged?

Not all tax debts are dischargeable in bankruptcy. To qualify for discharge, income tax debts must pass ALL of the following tests:

3-Year Rule: The tax return was due at least 3 years before filing (including extensions)

2-Year Rule: The tax return was actually filed at least 2 years before filing bankruptcy

240-Day Rule: The tax was assessed at least 240 days before filing

No fraud or willful evasion: The return was not fraudulent and you did not willfully attempt to evade the tax

Return was filed: You actually filed the tax return (unfiled returns cannot be discharged)

Priority vs. Non-Priority Tax Debt

Priority Tax Debt (NOT Dischargeable)

Recent income taxes (within 3 years)

Trust fund taxes (payroll taxes withheld from employees)

Taxes assessed within 240 days

Must be paid in full in Chapter 13

Survives Chapter 7 discharge

Non-Priority Tax Debt (Dischargeable)

Income taxes meeting the 3/2/240-day tests

Taxes on returns filed without fraud

Eliminated in Chapter 7 discharge

Treated as general unsecured in Chapter 13

May pay only a fraction through the plan

Delaware Tax Considerations

Delaware has a graduated state income tax with rates from 0% to 6.6%.

Delaware state income taxes generally follow the same discharge rules as federal taxes. To discharge Delaware state tax debt, the same 3-year, 2-year, and 240-day tests must be met.

Tax liens: Both federal and state tax liens survive bankruptcy. The lien remains on property you owned at the time of filing, even though your personal liability is discharged. This means the IRS or state can still collect from that specific property, but cannot pursue you personally for the debt.

Tax Debt Bankruptcy Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy discharge tax debt?

Yes, certain tax debts can be discharged in bankruptcy if they meet specific age and filing requirements. Income taxes that are more than 3 years old, were filed on time (or more than 2 years ago if filed late), and were assessed more than 240 days ago may be dischargeable.

What is the 3-year rule for tax debt in bankruptcy?

The tax return must have been due at least 3 years before the bankruptcy filing date (including extensions). For example, a 2022 tax return due April 15, 2023 would meet the 3-year rule after April 15, 2026.

What is the 2-year rule for tax debt?

If you filed the tax return late, it must have been filed at least 2 years before the bankruptcy filing. If you never filed the return, the tax debt cannot be discharged.

What is the 240-day rule?

The IRS must have assessed the tax at least 240 days before the bankruptcy filing. Assessment typically occurs when you file your return or when the IRS completes an audit. Certain events (like an offer in compromise) can toll this period.

What about Delaware state taxes?

Delaware state income taxes follow similar rules to federal taxes for bankruptcy discharge. Delaware has a graduated state income tax with rates from 0% to 6.6%. State tax liens, like federal tax liens, survive bankruptcy even if the underlying tax debt is discharged.

What happens to tax liens in bankruptcy?

Tax liens survive bankruptcy even if the underlying tax debt is discharged. The lien remains attached to property you owned at the time of filing. However, the personal obligation to pay is eliminated, so the IRS can only collect from the specific property to which the lien attached.

Alabama
Alaska
Arizona
Arkansas

## Tax Refunds and Chapter 13 in Delaware - Trustee Practices, Protecting Your Refund
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/tax-refund-chapter13.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Tax Refunds and Chapter 13 in Delaware

Tax Refunds and Chapter 13 in Delaware

In Chapter 13, the trustee may require you to turn over all or part of your tax refund as additional plan payments. Trustee practices vary by district, and there are strategies to protect some or all of your refund.

Quick answer: Most Chapter 13 trustees require debtors to turn over tax refunds above a threshold amount (typically $1,500-$2,500, but this varies by district). Your plan confirmation order or the trustee's standing instructions will specify requirements. Adjusting W-4 withholding to reduce the refund is one strategy, but must be done carefully to avoid bad-faith arguments.

Why the Trustee Wants Your Tax Refund

In Chapter 13, you commit all projected disposable income to your repayment plan for 3-5 years (11 U.S.C. section 1325(b)). The trustee's position is that a tax refund represents income that was over-withheld during the year - money that should have been available to pay creditors.

The logic: if you receive a $4,000 refund, that is $333/month in excess withholding that was not included in your disposable income calculation. The trustee argues this should go to your creditors.

Delaware Trustee Practices

Delaware has a state income tax (graduated, up to 6.6%). Both federal and state refunds are at risk in bankruptcy. The Chapter 13 trustee in Delaware typically requires turnover of tax refunds above a reasonable threshold. The federal wildcard exemption (if federal exemptions chosen) can protect refund amounts. Delaware has no specific tax refund exemption.

Strategies to Protect Your Refund

1. Adjust Your W-4 Withholding

The most effective strategy is to adjust your W-4 so you get more per paycheck and a smaller refund. If your refund is $500, the trustee has little to claim. Do this gradually and early in the plan - a sudden, dramatic change may trigger scrutiny.

2. Plan for the Refund in Your Budget

If you know the trustee will require turnover above $2,000, plan your withholding so your refund falls below that threshold. Use the IRS withholding estimator (irs.gov) to calibrate.

3. Request Permission to Keep the Refund

Some trustees allow debtors to keep refunds for specific purposes: necessary car repairs, medical expenses, or home maintenance. File a motion or request with the trustee explaining the need. Documentation helps (repair estimates, medical bills).

4. Earned Income Credit Arguments

If a significant portion of your refund is the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) or Child Tax Credit, some courts treat these as government benefits rather than disposable income. This argument has mixed results but is worth raising.

What Happens If You Do Not Turn Over the Refund

Failing to turn over a required tax refund can have serious consequences:

Motion to dismiss: The trustee can file a motion to dismiss your Chapter 13 case

Conversion to Chapter 7: The trustee or court may convert your case to Chapter 7

Bad faith finding: Repeated failure can be considered bad faith, affecting your ability to refile

Plan modification: The trustee may seek to modify your plan to increase payments

If you cannot turn over the refund or need to use it for an emergency, file a motion with the court BEFORE spending it. Courts are more sympathetic when you ask permission than when you ask forgiveness.

Frequently Asked Questions

Will the trustee take my tax refund?

Most likely some or all of it, depending on the amount and your district's practices. Check your plan terms for the specific threshold.

Can I adjust my withholding?

Yes, within reason. Gradual adjustments to reduce over-withholding are generally acceptable. Dramatic changes may be scrutinized.

What about Earned Income Credit?

Some courts treat EITC differently from regular refund amounts. This is not settled law everywhere - results vary by district.

When must I turn over the refund?

Typically within 30-60 days of filing your return. Check the trustee's standing instructions or your plan confirmation order.

What if I spent the refund already?

Contact your attorney immediately. The trustee may file a motion to dismiss. Being proactive about communicating with the trustee is better than ignoring the requirement.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

## Bankruptcy Timeline in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/timeline.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Timeline

Bankruptcy Timeline in Delaware

Step-by-step timeline for filing bankruptcy in Delaware. From credit counseling to discharge, what happens and when in the District of Delaware.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-4: Preparation

Gather financial documents: tax returns (2 years), pay stubs (6 months), bank statements (6 months), vehicle titles, mortgage statements, list of all debts. Complete required credit counseling course.

Day 1: Filing

Petition filed with the District of Delaware. Automatic stay takes effect immediately. All creditor collection stops. Case number and trustee assigned.

Days 30-40: 341 Meeting

Meeting of creditors at 824 Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801. Trustee asks questions about your finances under oath. Typically lasts 5-10 minutes. Bring photo ID and Social Security card.

Days 40-60: Debtor Education

Complete the second required course (debtor education / financial management). Must be completed before discharge can be entered.

Days 90-120: Discharge

Court enters discharge order approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting. All dischargeable debts are eliminated. The discharge injunction permanently bars creditors from collecting.

Days 120-150: Case Closed

Trustee files final report. Case is officially closed. Chapter 7 remains on credit report for 10 years from filing date.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Weeks 1-4: Preparation

Gather financial documents and complete credit counseling. Attorney drafts repayment plan based on your income, expenses, and debts.

Day 1: Filing

Petition and proposed plan filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Must begin plan payments within 30 days of filing.

Days 30-40: 341 Meeting

Meeting of creditors. Trustee reviews your plan and finances. Creditors may file objections.

Months 2-4: Plan Confirmation

Court hearing to confirm your repayment plan. Objections are resolved. Judge approves (or modifies) the plan.

Years 1-5: Plan Payments

Make monthly plan payments to the Chapter 13 trustee for 3-5 years. Trustee distributes payments to creditors. Stay current on mortgage and car payments.

Plan Completion: Discharge

After completing all plan payments and the debtor education course, the court enters your discharge. Any remaining unsecured debt balances are eliminated.

Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does Chapter 7 take in Delaware?

Chapter 7 in the District of Delaware typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. The 341 meeting happens about 30-40 days after filing. The discharge is entered approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting.

How long does Chapter 13 take in Delaware?

Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. Plan confirmation usually happens 2-4 months after filing. After completing all plan payments, the discharge is entered. The entire process takes 3-5 years.

What happens at the 341 meeting of creditors?

The 341 meeting (also called the meeting of creditors) is a brief hearing where the bankruptcy trustee and any creditors can ask you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and bankruptcy petition. In the District of Delaware, these meetings typically last 5-10 minutes.

How long before I can rebuild my credit?

You can start rebuilding credit immediately after discharge. Most people qualify for secured credit cards right away. FHA mortgages are available 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge. Conventional mortgages require a 4-year wait.

When does the automatic stay take effect?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant your bankruptcy petition is filed with the court. All creditor collection activity must stop immediately. Electronic filing means most cases are filed and effective within minutes.

How long does it take to prepare a bankruptcy filing?

Gathering documents and preparing the petition typically takes 1-4 weeks. You need tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements, a list of all debts and assets, and completion of the required credit counseling course.

## Timeshares and Bankruptcy in Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/timeshare.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Timeshares

Timeshares and Bankruptcy in Delaware

Trapped in a timeshare? Bankruptcy may be the most reliable exit. You can surrender the timeshare and discharge the mortgage, maintenance fees, and assessments.

Quick answer: You can surrender a timeshare in Chapter 7 bankruptcy and discharge all associated debts - mortgage, maintenance fees, assessments, and special levies. This is often faster and cheaper than timeshare exit companies.

Chapter 7: The Clean Exit

Chapter 7 is the most straightforward way to exit a timeshare:

List the timeshare as property on Schedule A/B (real or personal property, depending on how the timeshare is structured)

Indicate surrender on your Statement of Intention (Schedule D/Form 108)

Discharge the debt: The mortgage balance, maintenance fees, assessments, and any other obligations are discharged

Deed transfer: The timeshare company typically handles transferring the deed out of your name after discharge

Important: You are responsible for maintenance fees that accrue between filing and the date the timeshare is actually transferred. Work with the trustee to expedite the surrender.

Chapter 13: Keep or Surrender

Chapter 13 gives you more options:

Surrender through plan: Include the timeshare surrender in your Chapter 13 plan. Pre-petition arrears are treated as unsecured debt, paid at the plan percentage (often 10-30 cents on the dollar).

Keep and pay: If you want to keep the timeshare, continue paying the mortgage and fees. Include any arrears in the plan.

Lien stripping: If the timeshare is worth less than what you owe, you may be able to strip the lien in some circumstances - though this is more common with second mortgages on primary residences.

What Debts Are Covered?

Timeshare mortgage: Discharged in Chapter 7, paid through plan in Chapter 13

Maintenance fees (past due): Discharged as unsecured debt

Special assessments: Discharged as unsecured debt

Exchange program fees: Discharged (RCI, Interval International, etc.)

Collection agency debts: If the timeshare company sold the debt, the buyer's claim is also discharged

Bankruptcy vs. Timeshare Exit Companies

Bankruptcy

Filing fee: $338 (Chapter 7)

Legally binding discharge

Eliminates ALL timeshare debts

Process takes 3-4 months

Court-supervised, transparent

Also eliminates other qualifying debts

Exit Companies

Typical cost: $3,000-$10,000+

No legal guarantee of results

High rate of failure/scams

Process can take 12-24 months

No regulatory oversight

Only addresses the timeshare

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I get rid of a timeshare through bankruptcy in Delaware?

Yes. In Chapter 7, you can surrender a timeshare and discharge the remaining obligations - mortgage balance, maintenance fees, and assessments. The timeshare interest is listed as property of the estate and abandoned or surrendered through the bankruptcy process.

Will bankruptcy stop timeshare maintenance fees?

Bankruptcy discharges your personal liability for pre-petition maintenance fees. However, maintenance fees that accrue after filing are your responsibility until the timeshare is actually transferred out of your name. Surrendering in bankruptcy accelerates that process.

What happens to a timeshare mortgage in bankruptcy?

A timeshare mortgage is treated like any other secured debt. In Chapter 7, you can surrender the timeshare and discharge the debt. In Chapter 13, you can either keep paying it or surrender it through the plan. The lender may still foreclose on the timeshare interest itself.

Can I keep my timeshare in Delaware bankruptcy?

Yes. If you want to keep the timeshare, you can reaffirm the debt (Chapter 7) or continue paying through your plan (Chapter 13). You must stay current on mortgage payments, maintenance fees, and assessments.

Are timeshare exit companies legitimate?

Many timeshare exit companies charge thousands of dollars and deliver no results. Bankruptcy is often a more reliable and less expensive way to exit a timeshare. A Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 - far less than most exit company fees.

Does the timeshare HOA get paid in bankruptcy?

Pre-petition HOA/maintenance fees are unsecured debt and typically receive little or nothing in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, they are paid at the same rate as other unsecured creditors - often pennies on the dollar.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment

## Can I Travel During Chapter 13 in Delaware?
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/travel-chapter-13.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeWilmingtonChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Travel in Ch. 13

Can I Travel During Chapter 13 in Delaware?

Domestic travel is fine. International travel may require notifying your trustee. The real issue is not where you go - it is what you spend.

Quick answer: Domestic travel during Chapter 13 in Delaware is unrestricted. For international travel, notify your trustee as a precaution. The key rule: do not spend money on luxury travel while your creditors are being paid less than they are owed.

Domestic vs. International Travel

Domestic Travel

No restrictions or permissions needed

No notification to trustee required

Keep spending reasonable

Budget travel is generally fine

Work travel is never an issue

International Travel

No legal prohibition, but notify your trustee

Some trustees want advance notice

Extended trips may raise questions

Keep records of purpose and expenses

Luxury international travel is risky

What Could Cause Problems

Falling behind on plan payments: If you travel and then miss a plan payment, the trustee will connect the dots. This is the fastest way to get your case dismissed.

Lavish spending: Posting photos from expensive resorts while paying creditors 10 cents on the dollar creates a credibility problem. Trustees and creditors can review social media.

Unexplained income: If you are traveling frequently on a budget that says you have no discretionary income, expect questions about undisclosed income or assets.

Extended international trips: Spending months abroad can suggest you have income or assets not disclosed in your schedules.

The safest approach: keep travel modest, stay current on plan payments, and notify your trustee before any international travel.

What Is Always Fine

Work travel: Required by your employer, fully reimbursed - no issue whatsoever

Family emergencies: Travel for funerals, medical emergencies, or family crises

Local/regional day trips: Taking the kids to a state park or visiting family

Budget vacations: A modest road trip or camping trip within your means

Medical travel: Traveling for medical treatment or appointments

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I travel during Chapter 13 in Delaware?

Yes. Domestic travel is generally not restricted during Chapter 13 in Delaware or any other state. International travel may require notifying your trustee, depending on local practice. The key concern is whether travel spending conflicts with your budget and plan obligations.

Do I need court permission to travel during Chapter 13?

For domestic travel, no court permission is needed. For international travel, some trustees and courts want advance notice. There is no statute requiring permission, but your plan requires you to devote all disposable income to creditors - luxury travel could raise issues.

Can the trustee stop me from traveling?

The trustee cannot directly stop you from traveling. However, if you spend money on non-essential travel and then fall behind on plan payments, the trustee can file a motion to dismiss your case. Lavish travel spending can also trigger a motion to modify your plan upward.

Is international travel allowed during Chapter 13?

There is no law prohibiting international travel during Chapter 13. However, best practice is to notify your trustee before international trips. Some trustees view international travel as a sign that you have more disposable income than reported.

What about work travel during Chapter 13?

Business travel is not an issue. If your employer requires travel, that is a legitimate expense. Keep records showing the travel was work-related, especially if questioned by the trustee.

Can I take a vacation during Chapter 13?

Modest vacations are generally acceptable. The concern is extravagant spending while creditors are receiving less than what they are owed. A reasonable road trip or modest vacation is unlikely to cause problems. A cruise or international resort vacation may attract scrutiny.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
