## Stop Wage Garnishment in Delaware with Bankruptcy
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/wage-garnishment.html

Delaware Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Wage Garnishment

Stop Wage Garnishment in Delaware

How bankruptcy stops wage garnishment in Delaware. Delaware garnishment limits, the automatic stay, and recovering garnished wages.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Delaware Garnishment Laws

Garnishment limit: 15% of disposable earnings - more protective than the federal standard of 25%

Garnishment can take a significant portion of your paycheck, making it difficult to cover basic expenses like rent, utilities, and food. Filing bankruptcy provides immediate relief through the automatic stay.

How Bankruptcy Stops Garnishment

Garnishment Stopped By Bankruptcy

Credit card debt garnishment

Medical bill garnishment

Personal loan garnishment

Deficiency balance garnishment (repossession, foreclosure)

Old utility bill garnishment

Judgment debt garnishment

Garnishment NOT Stopped

Child support - domestic support obligations continue

Alimony - spousal support continues

Student loans - unless undue hardship is shown

Certain tax debts - priority tax obligations

Criminal restitution - court-ordered restitution

Recovering Garnished Wages

If wages were garnished within 90 days before your bankruptcy filing and the total exceeds $600, the bankruptcy trustee may recover those wages as a preferential transfer under 11 U.S.C. section 547. These recovered funds go into the bankruptcy estate but may benefit you in certain circumstances.

In Chapter 13, your repayment plan accounts for garnished wages and may provide additional recovery options.

Wage Garnishment Guide
Free Discharge Screener

Frequently Asked Questions

How much of my wages can be garnished in Delaware?

In Delaware, creditors can garnish 15% of disposable earnings - more protective than the federal standard of 25%. Filing bankruptcy immediately stops all garnishment through the automatic stay.

Does bankruptcy stop wage garnishment immediately?

Yes. The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362 takes effect. Your employer must stop withholding garnished wages once notified. Most courts provide electronic notice within 24-48 hours of filing.

Can I get back wages that were garnished?

In some cases, yes. If wages were garnished within 90 days before filing and exceed $600, the trustee may be able to recover them as a preferential transfer under 11 U.S.C. section 547. In Chapter 13, you may be able to recover garnished wages through your plan.

What types of garnishment can bankruptcy stop?

Bankruptcy stops garnishment for credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and most other unsecured debts. It does NOT stop garnishment for child support, alimony, most student loans (unless undue hardship is shown), and certain tax debts.

How long does it take to stop garnishment after filing in Delaware?

The automatic stay takes effect instantly upon filing. Most employers in the District of Delaware stop garnishment within 1-3 business days of receiving notice from the court. If garnishment continues after notice, that is a stay violation.

What if my employer continues garnishing after I file?

Continued garnishment after bankruptcy filing is a violation of the automatic stay. Document the violation and notify your attorney. You may be entitled to damages under section 362(k). Courts in the District of Delaware take stay violations seriously.

## 341 Meeting of Creditors in Wilmington, Delaware - What to Expect
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/wilmington/341-meeting.html

Wilmington Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Wilmington›341 Meeting

341 Meeting of Creditors in Wilmington

The 341 meeting is a brief, required hearing in every bankruptcy case. Here is what Wilmington residents need to know.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Happens at the 341 Meeting

About 30 days after filing, you attend a meeting of creditors (named after Section 341 of the Bankruptcy Code). Despite the name, creditors rarely attend. The bankruptcy trustee runs the meeting.

1Identity verification

Bring a government-issued photo ID and proof of Social Security number (card, tax return, or W-2).

2Oath

You are placed under oath. All answers must be truthful.

3Trustee questions

The trustee asks about your petition, assets, debts, income, and recent transactions. Most questions confirm what is already in your paperwork.

4Creditor questions (rare)

Creditors may attend and ask questions, but this is uncommon in consumer cases.

Meeting Location

District: D. Del.

Courthouse: 824 Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801

Some 341 meetings may be held by telephone or video conference. Your attorney will confirm the format and location.

What to Bring

Government-issued photo ID (driver's license, passport, or state ID)

Proof of Social Security number (card, W-2, or tax return)

Recent pay stubs (last 60 days)

Most recent tax return

Bank statements (last 3-6 months)

Vehicle titles or registration

Any documents your attorney requests

Frequently Asked Questions

What is a 341 meeting?

The 341 meeting of creditors is a brief hearing where the bankruptcy trustee asks you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and debts. It is required in every bankruptcy case.

Where is the 341 meeting held in Wilmington?

341 meetings for the D. Del. are typically held at or near the courthouse at 824 Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801. Some meetings may be conducted by phone or video.

What questions are asked at the 341 meeting?

The trustee will verify your identity, confirm the accuracy of your petition, ask about your assets and debts, and inquire about recent financial transactions.

How long does the 341 meeting last?

Most 341 meetings last 5-10 minutes. If your case is straightforward and your paperwork is complete, it should be brief.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Wilmington, Delaware - Repayment Plans
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/wilmington/chapter-13.html

Wilmington Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Wilmington›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Wilmington, Delaware

Chapter 13 lets you reorganize your debts into a manageable 3-5 year repayment plan while keeping your property. Here is how it works for Wilmington residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Chapter 13 Works

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not eliminate debts immediately. Instead, you propose a repayment plan to pay back some or all of your debts over 3-5 years. Key benefits:

Keep your home - catch up on missed mortgage payments through the plan

Keep your car - restructure car loan payments, potentially at a lower interest rate

No means test - income determines plan length, not eligibility

Protect co-signers - the co-debtor stay prevents creditors from pursuing co-signers

Cram down car loans - if your car loan is older than 910 days, you may reduce the balance to the car's value

Plan Length and Payments

Your plan length depends on your income relative to the Delaware median:

Income LevelPlan LengthPayment Basis

Below Delaware median3 yearsDisposable income after allowed expenses

Above Delaware median5 yearsDisposable income after allowed expenses

Your monthly payment is based on your disposable income - the amount left after subtracting necessary living expenses from your income. Unsecured creditors receive whatever your disposable income can pay over the plan period.

Chapter 13 Timeline in Wilmington

1File petition and proposed plan

Your attorney files your petition and proposed repayment plan with the D. Del.. The automatic stay stops all collection actions.

2Begin plan payments (30 days)

You must begin making plan payments within 30 days of filing, even before the plan is confirmed.

3341 Meeting of Creditors (30-45 days)

Brief meeting where the trustee reviews your finances and plan.

4Confirmation hearing (2-3 months)

The court approves or modifies your plan. Creditors may object.

5Complete plan payments (3-5 years)

Make all plan payments through the trustee. Remaining qualifying unsecured debts are discharged.

Frequently Asked Questions

How does Chapter 13 work in Wilmington?

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years through a court-approved plan. Cases are filed in the D. Del..

How long is a Chapter 13 plan?

If your income is below the Delaware median, your plan lasts 3 years. If above, it lasts 5 years.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. Chapter 13 allows you to catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan while keeping your home.

What is the Chapter 13 dismissal rate?

Chapter 13 dismissal rates vary. Nationally, about 33% of Chapter 13 cases are dismissed before completion. Working with an experienced attorney improves your chances of success.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Wilmington, Delaware - Eliminate Debt
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/wilmington/chapter-7.html

Wilmington Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Wilmington›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Wilmington, Delaware

Chapter 7 is the fastest form of bankruptcy relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is how it works for Wilmington residents filing in the D. Del..

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7 bankruptcy (also called "liquidation" bankruptcy) eliminates most unsecured debts - credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. In exchange, non-exempt assets may be sold to pay creditors. However, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

In Delaware, you can protect up to $125,000 in home equity and $15,000 in vehicle equity. Additional exemptions cover household goods, clothing, retirement accounts, and more.

Do You Qualify? The Means Test

To file Chapter 7 in Delaware, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the Delaware median.

Household SizeAnnual Median Income

1 person$63,057

2 persons$80,238

3 persons$96,649

4 persons$115,946

If your income is below the median for your household size, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed expense calculation may still allow you to file.

Chapter 7 Timeline in Wilmington

1File petition with the D. Del.

Your attorney files your petition, schedules, and means test. The automatic stay immediately stops collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

2341 Meeting of Creditors (30 days)

You attend a brief meeting of creditors where the trustee asks questions under oath about your finances.

3Discharge (60-90 days after 341)

If no objections are filed, you receive your discharge order eliminating qualifying debts.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans

Old utility bills

Most old tax debts

Deficiency balances

Not Dischargeable

Student loans (usually)

Child support / alimony

Recent tax debts

Debts from fraud

DUI injury debts

Government fines

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I qualify for Chapter 7 in Wilmington?

You must pass the means test. If your household income is below the Delaware median, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed expense calculation determines eligibility.

How long does Chapter 7 take in Wilmington?

Typically 3-4 months from filing to discharge. The 341 meeting of creditors is usually 30 days after filing.

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not eliminate student loans (usually), child support, alimony, most tax debts, or debts from fraud.

Will I lose my car in Chapter 7 in Delaware?

Delaware's vehicle exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity in your car. If your car equity is below that amount, you keep it.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Wilmington, Delaware?
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/wilmington/cost.html

Wilmington Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Wilmington›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Wilmington?

A complete breakdown of filing fees, attorney costs, and other expenses for bankruptcy in Wilmington, Delaware.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Fees

ChapterCourt Filing FeeTypical Attorney FeeTotal Estimated Cost

Chapter 7$338$1,200 to $2,500$1,538 - $2,538+

Chapter 13$313$3,500 to $5,500$3,813 - $5,313+

Attorney fees vary based on case complexity. Chapter 13 attorney fees are typically paid through your repayment plan.

Additional Costs

ExpenseCostNotes

Credit counseling (pre-filing)$10 - $25Required before filing

Debtor education (post-filing)$10 - $25Required before discharge

Credit report$0 - $30Free annually from each bureau

Document preparationVariesUsually included in attorney fee

Fee Waivers and Free Legal Aid

If your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you may qualify for a fee waiver (Chapter 7 only). You can also request to pay the filing fee in installments.

Free legal aid in Delaware:

Delaware Volunteer Legal Services

Community Legal Aid Society

Visit howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com for a detailed national cost guide.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Wilmington?

The filing fee is $338. Attorney fees typically range from $1,200 to $2,500. You also need two credit counseling courses ($25-$50 total).

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Wilmington?

The filing fee is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $3,500 to $5,500. Most Chapter 13 attorney fees can be paid through your plan.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

If your income is below 150% of the poverty line, you can apply to have the filing fee waived. You may also qualify for free legal aid services.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Delaware Bankruptcy Exemptions - What You Can Keep in Wilmington
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/wilmington/exemptions.html

Wilmington Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Wilmington›Exemptions

Delaware Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you can keep when filing bankruptcy. Delaware has specific exemption amounts that protect your home, car, and personal property.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Key Delaware Exemptions

Property TypeExemption AmountDetails

Homestead$125,000Equity in your primary residence

Vehicle$15,000Equity in one motor vehicle

Wildcard$5,000Applied to any property

Household goodsVariesFurniture, appliances, clothing

Retirement accountsUnlimited401(k), IRA, pension (ERISA-qualified)

Social SecurityUnlimitedFully exempt under federal law

Tools of tradeVariesEquipment needed for your job

Note: Delaware allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions. Federal exemptions may be more favorable depending on your situation.

How Exemptions Work

Exemptions protect the equity in your property, not its total value. Equity is what your property is worth minus what you owe on it.

Example: If your car is worth $10,000 and you owe $7,000 on the loan, you have $3,000 in equity. If Delaware's vehicle exemption covers that equity, you keep the car.

In Chapter 7, non-exempt property may be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, you keep all property but must pay unsecured creditors at least the value of your non-exempt assets over your plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the homestead exemption in Delaware?

Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 of equity in your primary residence.

What is the vehicle exemption in Delaware?

Delaware's vehicle exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity in your car, truck, or motorcycle.

Can I choose federal exemptions in Delaware?

Yes. Delaware allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## Bankruptcy FAQ - Common Questions for Wilmington, Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/wilmington/faq.html

Wilmington Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Wilmington›FAQ

Bankruptcy FAQ for Wilmington, Delaware

Answers to the most common questions about filing bankruptcy in Wilmington.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file bankruptcy in Wilmington?

You file a bankruptcy petition with the D. Del.. Most people hire an attorney, though you can file pro se. Before filing, you must complete a credit counseling course.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 in Wilmington?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 lets you repay debts over 3-5 years while keeping property. Your income, assets, and goals determine the best choice.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay takes effect immediately when you file and stops all garnishments, lawsuits, and collection calls.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit report?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people see their credit scores begin to recover within 1-2 years of filing.

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy in Delaware?

Delaware's vehicle exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity. If your equity is within the exemption, you keep the car. In Chapter 13, you continue making payments through your plan.

Can I keep my house if I file bankruptcy in Delaware?

Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 of equity. In Chapter 13, you can catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan.

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical debt is unsecured debt that is dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy.

Do I have to go to court?

You must attend the 341 meeting of creditors, which is usually held at a meeting room (not a courtroom). Your attorney handles most court appearances.

Can I file bankruptcy if I am married?

Yes. You can file individually or jointly with your spouse. Filing jointly saves on attorney fees and filing costs.

Can I file bankruptcy again?

After a Chapter 7 discharge, wait 8 years for another Chapter 7 or 4 years for Chapter 13. After a Chapter 13 discharge, wait 2 years for another Chapter 13 or 6 years for Chapter 7. Use the 1328(f) screener at 1328f.com to check eligibility.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/wilmington/

Wilmington Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Filing Bankruptcy in Wilmington, Delaware

Free 2026 guide to bankruptcy in Wilmington. Covers Chapter 7, Chapter 13, Delaware exemptions, costs, and what to expect when filing in the D. Del..

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

$125,000

Homestead Exemption

$15,000

Vehicle Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Explore Wilmington Bankruptcy Topics

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy

Learn how Chapter 7 works in Wilmington. Wipe out most unsecured debt in 3-4 months.

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy

Repay debts over 3-5 years while keeping your property. How Chapter 13 works in Wilmington.

Delaware Exemptions

What you can keep: homestead ($125,000), vehicle ($15,000), and more.

Cost of Filing

Filing fees, attorney costs, and credit counseling fees for Wilmington residents.

Frequently Asked Questions

Common questions about filing bankruptcy in Wilmington, Delaware.

Means Test

Do you qualify for Chapter 7? Delaware median income thresholds and the means test calculation.

341 Meeting of Creditors

What to expect at the 341 meeting in Wilmington. Location, preparation, and common questions.

Courthouse Information

District: D. Del.

Courthouse: 824 Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801

Legal Aid: Delaware Volunteer Legal Services, Community Legal Aid Society

You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Nearby Cities

Bankruptcy information for communities near Wilmington, Delaware.

Newark

New Castle County · Pop. 34,000

Dover

Kent County · Pop. 39,000

Middletown

New Castle County · Pop. 25,000

Bear

New Castle County · Pop. 22,000

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Wilmington, Delaware?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Wilmington typically range from $1,200 to $2,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,500 to $5,500 for Chapter 13.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Wilmington?

Delaware's homestead exemption protects up to $125,000 of equity in your home. If your equity is below that amount, you can keep your home in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you keep your home while catching up on missed payments.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Wilmington?

Bankruptcy cases in Wilmington are filed in the D. Del.. The courthouse is located at 824 Market St, Wilmington, DE 19801.

How long does bankruptcy take in Wilmington?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan before receiving a discharge.

Check Your Eligibility

## Wilmington Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7? - Delaware
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/wilmington/means-test.html

Wilmington Bankruptcy Guide
Home Chapter 7 Chapter 13 Exemptions Cost FAQ Means Test 341 Meeting State Guide OBP

Home›Delaware Bankruptcy Guide›Wilmington›Means Test

Wilmington Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?

The means test determines whether you can file Chapter 7 bankruptcy. Your income is compared to the Delaware median for your household size.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

2026 Delaware Median Income Thresholds

Household SizeAnnual Median Income

1 person$63,057

2 persons$80,238

3 persons$96,649

4 persons$115,946

Each additional personAdd approximately $9,900

If your annualized income is below the median for your household size, you pass the means test automatically and qualify for Chapter 7.

Step-by-Step Means Test Walkthrough

1Calculate your current monthly income (CMI)

Add up all income from all sources for the six full calendar months before filing. Divide by six for monthly average. Multiply by 12 for annualized figure.

2Determine your household size

Count everyone who lives in your household and depends on your income - yourself, spouse, and dependent children.

3Compare to Delaware median income

If your annualized CMI is at or below the median, you pass. No further calculation needed.

4If above median: Calculate disposable income

Subtract IRS-approved living expenses, secured debt payments, and priority debts. If remaining disposable income (x60) is less than $9,075, you pass.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the median income for the means test in Delaware in 2026?

The 2026 Delaware median income thresholds are: 1-person household $63,057, 2-person $80,238, 3-person $96,649, 4-person $115,946. Add approximately $9,900 for each additional household member.

What happens if I fail the means test?

You can file Chapter 13 instead (no means test), challenge with special circumstances, wait until your income drops, or re-examine deductions with an attorney.

Does the means test apply to Chapter 13?

No, but your income level determines whether your Chapter 13 plan lasts 3 years (below median) or 5 years (above median).

What income counts for the means test?

Average monthly income from all sources over the six full calendar months before filing. Includes wages, business income, rental income, pensions. Social Security is excluded from the initial median comparison.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

Bankruptcy Guide for Bear, Delaware

Bankruptcy information for residents of Bear (New Castle County). Bear filers use Delaware state exemptions and file in the D. Del..

Homestead Exemption

$15,000

Vehicle Exemption

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Filing Bankruptcy in Bear

Residents of Bear (New Castle County, population approximately 22,000) file bankruptcy in the D. Del.. The same Delaware exemptions apply to all filers in the state.

Key Delaware exemptions:

Homestead: $125,000 of equity in your home

Vehicle: $15,000 of equity in your car

Retirement: Unlimited for ERISA-qualified plans

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13

Chapter 7

Eliminate most unsecured debt

Takes 3-4 months

Must pass means test

Filing fee: $338

Chapter 13

Repay debts over 3-5 years

Keep all property

No means test

Filing fee: $313

Where do Bear residents file for bankruptcy?

Residents of Bear, Delaware file in the D. Del.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

How much does bankruptcy cost in Bear?

Filing fees are the same across Delaware: $338 for Chapter 7 and $313 for Chapter 13. Attorney fees in the Wilmington area typically range from $1,200 to $2,500 for Chapter 7.

What exemptions apply in Bear?

Bear residents use Delaware state exemptions: homestead $125,000, vehicle $15,000.

## Privacy Policy - delawarebankruptcy.org
Source: https://delawarebankruptcy.org/privacy.html

delawarebankruptcy.org

Home
Privacy

Privacy Policy

How we handle your data. Short version: we don't sell it, share it, or use it for advertising.

Data We Collect

We use Google Analytics (GA4) to measure aggregate site traffic. This includes page views, session duration, approximate geographic region (country and city level), and device type. We do not collect names, email addresses, or personally identifiable information through analytics.

Aggregate event data from Google Analytics is also exported to Google BigQuery for research use. The BigQuery data is used only for internal trajectory analysis and network health reports, is not shared with third parties, and is not used for advertising.

What We Don't Do

We do not sell data. We do not run advertising. We do not collect financial information. We do not use retargeting. We do not share any user data with attorneys, law firms, creditors, debt collectors, or legal service providers.

Specifically, we do not enable Google Signals, Google Ads integration, User-ID collection, or any cross-device tracking feature in Google Analytics. GA4 operates in aggregate-traffic-measurement mode only on this site.

Cookies

We use only first-party cookies set by Google Analytics for aggregate traffic measurement. We do not use advertising cookies, tracking pixels, or third-party analytics cookies.

Third-Party Services

Google Analytics (GA4) - aggregate traffic measurement

Google BigQuery - aggregate event data warehouse for research use only; not shared, not used for advertising

Google Search Console - search query measurement (clicks, impressions, our own page rankings)

GitHub Pages - hosting

Contact Forms & Email

This site may link to contact forms or email addresses operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project. Any data you submit through those channels is handled under the Open Bankruptcy Project master privacy policy, which governs all sites in this network.

Children's Privacy

This site is not directed at children under 13. We do not knowingly collect information from children.

Data Retention

Analytics data is retained for 14 months per Google's standard retention settings. After that window, individual event data is automatically deleted by Google; aggregate report metrics remain available.

Your Rights

You may request deletion of any personal data we hold by contacting info@openbankruptcyproject.org. California residents have additional rights under CCPA. EU residents have additional rights under GDPR. All such requests are handled by the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Network Policy

This site is part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit. The same privacy standards apply across every site in the network. The authoritative master policy is maintained at https://openbankruptcyproject.org/privacy.html.

Changes

We may update this policy from time to time. Changes are reflected on the master policy first and then mirrored across all network sites. Check the master policy for the most current version.

Effective: March 30, 2026  |  Updated: April 11, 2026

Home  ·  Privacy  ·  Open Bankruptcy Project

Part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network. Educational information only - not legal advice.

No ads. No affiliate links. No data sold. Supported by donations.
